title: EXPOSÉ VII REGULAR IMMERSIONS AND CALCULATION OF THE \(K'\) OF A BLOWN-UP SCHEME

pages.title: EXPOSÉ VII REGULAR IMMERSIONS AND CALCULATION OF THE \(K'\) OF A BLOWN-UP SCHEME

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ VII
REGULAR IMMERSIONS AND CALCULATION OF THE \(K'\) OF A BLOWN-UP SCHEMEby Pierre BERTHELOT
1. Generalities on regular immersions

The classical notion of regular immersion (EGA IV 16.9.2) proving inadequate in the non-noetherian case, we will define a weaker notion, which however gives rise to the desired properties, and which is equivalent to the usual definition in the noetherian case.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.1.

Let \(A\) be a commutative ring, \(E\) a projective \(A\)-module of finite type, \(u:E\longrightarrow A\) a \(A\)-linear homomorphism. We say that \(u\) is regular if the Koszul complex \(K_\bullet(u)\) defined by \(u\) is acyclic in dimension \(>0\), hence is a resolution of \(B=A/u(E)\).

Recall that the Koszul complex associated to \(u\) is defined as follows: the graded \(A\)-module \(K_\bullet(u)\) is the exterior algebra \(\Lambda(E)\), and the boundary operator is interior multiplication by \(u\) regarded as an element of the dual \(\check E\): it is an antiderivation of degree \(-1\) of \(\Lambda(E)\), equal to \(u\) on \(E\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.2.

Let \(u:E\longrightarrow A\) be a regular homomorphism. Let \(A'\) be a \(A\)-algebra, \(u':E'\longrightarrow A'\) the homomorphism deduced from \(u\) by base change; we assume \(u'\) regular. We set \(J=u(E)\), \(J'=JA'=u(E')\). Then we have the following relations for every integer \(n\geq0\):

\[ \begin{aligned} (1.2.1)\quad &J^n/J^{n+1}\otimes_A A'\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}J'^n/J'^{n+1}, &(1.2.2)\quad &\operatorname{Tor}_i^A(J^n/J^{n+1},A')=0\quad\text{pour }i\geq1;\\ (1.2.3)\quad &J^n\otimes_A A'\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}J'^n, &(1.2.4)\quad &\operatorname{Tor}_i^A(J^n,A')=0\quad\text{pour }i\geq1. \end{aligned} \]
a) We first assume that the \(J^n/J^{n+1}\) are projective over \(B=A/J\); since \(J\) is of finite type, they are locally free of finite type over \(B\).

The relations to be proved being verified pointwise on \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), we are reduced to the case \(A\) local, with \(u(E)\subset\operatorname{rad}(A)\). The \(J^n/J^{n+1}\) are then free over \(B\), and the relations \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^A(J^n/J^{n+1},A')=0\) for \(i\geq1\) are equivalent to the relations \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^A(B,A')=0\) for \(i\geq1\). Now we can compute the \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^A\) using the projective resolution \(K_\bullet(u)\) of \(B\); they are the homology groups of the complex \(K_\bullet(u)\otimes_A A'=K_\bullet(u')\). Since \(u'\) is assumed regular, the latter is acyclic in degrees \(>0\), whence the relations \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^A(J^n/J^{n+1},A')=0\), for \(i\geq1\).
We deduce the relations \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^A(A/J^n,A')=0\) for \(i\geq1\), proceeding by induction on \(n\) thanks to the exact sequence of Tor associated with the exact sequence:
\[ 0\longrightarrow J^n/J^{n+1}\longrightarrow A/J^{n+1}\longrightarrow A/J^n\longrightarrow0. \]

Since for every \(k\) we have \(J^kA'=J'^k\), we draw from this exact sequence and from \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(A/J^n,A')=0\) the relations \(J^n/J^{n+1}\otimes_A A'\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}J'^n/J'^{n+1}\).
From the exact sequence:
\[ 0\longrightarrow J^n\longrightarrow A\longrightarrow A/J^n\longrightarrow0, \]
and from \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(A/J^n,A')=0\), we deduce the relations \(J^n\otimes_A A'\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}J'^n\).
Finally, from the exact sequence of Tor associated with the exact sequence:
\[ 0\longrightarrow J^{n+1}\longrightarrow J^n\longrightarrow J^n/J^{n+1}\longrightarrow0, \]
we deduce the relations \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^A(J^n,A')=0\) for \(i\geq1\), by induction on \(n\), the case \(n=0\) being obvious.

b) We will now show that the \(J^n/J^{n+1}\) are projective over \(B=A/J\). The question being local on \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), we may assume \(E\) free, say \(E=(A)^d\), so that \(u\) is defined by a sequence \(f_1,\ldots,f_d\) of elements of \(A\). Let us set \(A_0=\mathbf Z[T_1,\ldots,T_d]\) and let \(A_0\longrightarrow A\) be the homomorphism which maps

\(T_i\) to \(f_i\); let us denote by \(u_0:E_0=(A_0)^d\longrightarrow A_0\) the homomorphism defined by the sequence \((T_1,\ldots,T_d)\), so that \(u\) is deduced from \(u_0\) by base change from \(A_0\) to \(A\). Since the sequence \((T_1,\ldots,T_d)\) is regular (in the classical sense), the homomorphism \(u_0\) is regular, and we are under the conditions of the lemma, applied to the base change \(A_0\longrightarrow A\). Now the \(J_0^n/J_0^{n+1}\) are free of finite type over \(\mathbf Z\), so we can apply the results of a). Consequently \(J^n/J^{n+1}\mathrel{\widetilde\longleftarrow}J_0^n/J_0^{n+1}\otimes_{A_0}A\), and \(J^n/J^{n+1}\) is free of finite type.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.3.

Let \(A\) be a ring, \(E\) a projective \(A\)-module of finite type, \(u:E\longrightarrow A\) a regular homomorphism, \(J=u(E)\). Then the ideal \(J\) is quasi-regular (EGA IV 16.9.7), that is (EGA IV 16.9.8):

i) \(J\) is of finite type.

ii) \(J/J^2\) is a projective \(A/J\)-module of finite type;

iii) the canonical homomorphism

\[ (1.3.1)\qquad \operatorname{Sym}_{A/J}(J/J^2)\longrightarrow \operatorname{gr}_J(A)=\bigoplus_{n\geq0}J^n/J^{n+1}, \]

is an isomorphism.

We saw in the proof of 1.2 b) that \(J/J^2\) is projective of finite type. Let us take up again the notations of 1.2 b) (the question being local on \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), we again assume \(E\) free of finite type). Then the canonical homomorphism (1.3.1) is deduced from the analogous homomorphism for \(J_0\) by the base change \(A_0\longrightarrow A\), by the relations 1.2. Now the latter is an isomorphism, hence the same holds for (1.3.1).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.4.

Let \((X,A)\) be a commutatively locally ringed site, \(E\) a locally free \(A\)-Module of finite type, \(u:E\longrightarrow A\) a \(A\)-linear homomorphism. We say that \(u\) is regular if the Koszul complex \(K_\bullet(u)\) defined by \(u\) is acyclic in degrees \(>0\). Let \(J\) be an Ideal of \(A\); we say that \(J\) is regular if, locally on \(X\), there exist a free \(A\)-Module of finite type \(E\), and a surjective regular homomorphism: \(E\longrightarrow J\).

Let \(i:Y\longrightarrow X\) be an immersion of preschemes, and let \(U\) be an open subset of \(X\) such that \(i(Y)\subset U\) and such that \(i\) is a closed immersion of \(Y\) into \(U\). We say that \(i\) is regular if the ideal of \(O_U\) defining \(i(Y)\) is regular (a condition which does not depend on \(U\)). If \(y\in Y\), we say that \(i\) is regular at \(y\) if there exists a neighborhood \(V\) of \(i(y)\) such that the immersion \(i^{-1}(V)\longrightarrow V\) is regular. In order that an immersion be regular, it is therefore necessary and sufficient that it be so in a neighborhood of each point.

This definition conflicts with EGA IV 16.9.2, and replaces the latter. However, in the noetherian case, the two definitions are equivalent (EGA IV 19.5.1). An immersion which can locally be defined by a regular sequence is regular; a regular immersion is quasi-regular (1.3).

1.4.1. Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(J\) an Ideal of finite type of \(O_X\), \(x\) a point of \(\operatorname{Supp}(O_X/J)\). We will say that a family of sections \(f_1,\ldots,f_d\) of \(J\) on a neighborhood of \(x\) forms a minimal system of generators of \(J\) in a neighborhood of \(x\) if the \((f_i)_x\) are a family of generators of \(J_x\) of minimal number, i.e. their images in \(J_x\otimes k(x)\) form a basis of this vector space over \(k(x)\). Note that the \(f_i\) generate \(J\) on a neighborhood of \(x\).

1.4.2. To say that an immersion is regular is also to say that it is defined in a certain open subset of \(X\) by an ideal of finite type \(J\), and that for every point \(x\in\operatorname{Supp}(O_X/J)\), and every minimal system of generators of \(J\) in a neighborhood of \(x\), the Koszul complex associated to this sequence is acyclic in a neighborhood of \(x\): indeed, let \(f_1,\ldots,f_d\) be a minimal system of generators of \(J\) in a neighborhood of \(x\) such that the associated Koszul complex is acyclic (one exists if we assume the immersion regular); let \(g_1,\ldots,g_d\) be another minimal system of generators of \(J\); there exists a neighborhood of \(x\) on which \(J\) can be generated either by the \(f_i\), or by the \(g_j\), and consequently, there exists on this neighborhood a matrix with coefficients in \(O_X\) allowing us to express the \(g_j\) in terms of the \(f_i\). Now the images of the \(f_i\) (resp. \(g_j\)) in \(J/J^2\) form a basis at the point \(x\), hence in a neighborhood of \(x\), and consequently this matrix is invertible in a neighborhood of \(x\). It therefore allows us to define an isomorphism from the Koszul complex defined by the \(g_j\) onto the Koszul complex defined by the \(f_i\), which shows that the Koszul complex defined by the \(g_j\) is acyclic.

1.4.3. Assuming only \(J\) of finite type, and \(J/J^2\) locally free of finite type, note that every minimal system of generators of \(J\) in a neighborhood of a point \(y\) of \(Y\) gives a basis of \(J/J^2\) in a neighborhood of \(y\). Conversely, if we have in a neighborhood of \(y\) a basis of \(J/J^2\), we can lift it in a neighborhood of \(y\) to a family of sections of \(J\), which generate \(J_y\) by Nakayama, hence \(J\) in a neighborhood of \(y\) since \(J\) is of finite type; moreover these generators are minimal in number, since they give a basis of \(J/J^2\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.5. (Compare EGA IV 19.1.5 (ii)).

Let \(i:Y\longrightarrow X\) be an immersion. If \(i\) is regular, the same holds for every immersion deduced from \(i\) by flat base change. In order that \(i\) be regular, it is necessary and sufficient that it be so locally for the faithfully flat quasi-compact topology.
The stability under flat base change is obvious.

Conversely, let \(i'\) be the immersion deduced from \(i\) by a faithfully flat quasi-compact base change. If \(i'\) is regular, then the Ideal \(J\) which defines \(i\) is of finite type, and \(J/J^2\) is locally free of finite type, since these properties descend by faithfully flat quasi-compact morphisms. If \(f_1,\ldots,f_d\) is a sequence of sections of \(J\) lifting a basis of \(J/J^2\), the Koszul complex associated to the inverse images of the \(f_i\) is acyclic in degrees \(>0\) by 1.4; since it is the inverse image of the Koszul complex associated to the \(f_i\), the latter is acyclic in degrees \(>0\), and \(i\) is regular.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.6.

Let \(i:Y\longrightarrow X\) be an immersion defined in an open subset \(U\) of \(X\) by an Ideal \(J\) of \(O_X\); the \(O_Y\)-Module \(J/J^2\) (independent of \(U\)) is called the conormal sheaf of \(Y\) in \(X\), and denoted \(N_{X/Y}\). If \(i\) is regular, \(J/J^2\) is locally free of finite type, and its rank is called the codimension of \(Y\) in \(X\), or also the codimension of \(i\).
If we have two closed immersions: \(Z\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^jY\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^iX\), the conormal sheaves form an exact sequence:
\[ (1.6.1)\qquad j^*(N_{X/Y})\longrightarrow N_{X/Z}\longrightarrow N_{Y/Z}\longrightarrow0. \]
Indeed, let \(I\) be the ideal of \(Y\) in \(X\), \(J\) the ideal of \(Z\) in \(X\), \(K\) the ideal of \(Z\) in \(Y\). We have the exact sequence:
\[ 0\longrightarrow I\longrightarrow J\longrightarrow K\longrightarrow0\,; \]
whence, by tensoring with \(O_X/I=O_Y\), the exact sequence:
\[ I/I^2\longrightarrow J\otimes_{O_X}O_Y\longrightarrow K\otimes_{O_X}O_Y\longrightarrow0\,; \]
tensoring with \(O_Z=O_X/J=O_Y/K\), we obtain the exact sequence:
\[ j^*(I/I^2)\longrightarrow J\otimes_{O_X}O_Z\longrightarrow K\otimes_{O_Y}O_Z\longrightarrow0, \]
which is the desired sequence.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.7. (Compare EGA IV 19.1.5 (iv)).

Let \(i:Y\longrightarrow X\), and \(j:Z\longrightarrow Y\) be two immersions:

i) If \(i\) and \(j\) are regular, \(i\circ j\) is regular, and the sequence:

\[ 0\longrightarrow j^*(N_{X/Y})\longrightarrow N_{X/Z}\longrightarrow N_{Y/Z}\longrightarrow0 \]

is exact; in particular, \(\operatorname{codim}(i\circ j)=\operatorname{codim}(i)+\operatorname{codim}(j)\).

ii) If \(i\circ j\) and \(i\) are regular, and if the sequence:

\[ 0\longrightarrow j^*(N_{X/Y})\longrightarrow N_{X/Z}\longrightarrow N_{Y/Z}\longrightarrow0, \]

is exact and locally split, \(j\) is regular;

iii) If \(X\) is noetherian, and if \(i\circ j\) and \(j\) are regular, \(i\) is regular at the points of \(Z\).

The question being local on \(X\), we may assume \(X\) affine and the immersions closed; let \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(B)\), \(Z=\operatorname{Spec}(C)\), with \(B=A/I\), \(C=A/J=B/K\).

i) Localizing if necessary, we may assume \(I/I^2\) free over \(B\), and \(K/K^2\) free over \(C\) (1.3). Let \(f_1,\ldots,f_n\) be elements of \(A\) lifting a basis of \(I/I^2\), \(g_1,\ldots,g_m\) elements of \(A\) lifting a basis of \(K/K^2\); then, localizing further if necessary, we may assume that \(f_1,\ldots,f_n\) generate \(I\), that \(f_1,\ldots,f_n,g_1,\ldots,g_m\) generate \(J\), and that \(\bar g_1,\ldots,\bar g_m\) generate \(K\), \(\bar g_j\) being the image of \(g_j\) in \(B\) (1.4); moreover, \(f_1,\ldots,f_n\) form a minimal system of generators of \(I\), and \(\bar g_1,\ldots,\bar g_m\) a minimal system of generators of \(K\).
We consider the diagram:

\[ (1.7.1)\qquad \begin{array}{ccccc} A_0=\mathbf Z[T_1,\ldots,T_{n+m}]&\longrightarrow&A\\ \downarrow&&\downarrow\\ B_0=\mathbf Z[T_1,\ldots,T_m]&\longrightarrow&B\\ \downarrow&&\downarrow\\ C_0=\mathbf Z&\longrightarrow&C, \end{array} \]

where the horizontal arrows are defined by sending \(T_i\) to \(f_i\) for \(1\leq i\leq n\), and \(T_{n+j}\) to \(g_j\) for \(1\leq j\leq m\), where the homomorphism \(A_0\longrightarrow B_0\) is defined by sending \(T_i\) to 0 for \(1\leq i\leq n\), and \(T_{n+j}\) to \(T_j\) for \(1\leq j\leq m\), and the homomorphism \(B_0\longrightarrow C_0\) by annihilating the \(T_j\), \(1\leq j\leq m\). We then have:
\[B=A\otimes_{A_0}B_0,\qquad C=A\otimes_{A_0}C_0=B\otimes_{B_0}C_0.\]

The situation studied is therefore deduced from the situation \((A_0,B_0,C_0)\) by the base change \(A_0\longrightarrow A\). Now the corresponding immersions \(i_0\), \(j_0\) and \(i_0\circ j_0\) are regular; consequently, the homology groups of the Koszul complexes associated to the sequences \((f_1,\ldots,f_n)\), \((f_1,\ldots,f_n,g_1,\ldots,g_m)\) and \((\bar g_1,\ldots,\bar g_m)\) are respectively the \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^{A_0}(B_0,A)\), \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^{A_0}(C_0,A)\), \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^{B_0}(C_0,B)\). Consequently:

\[\begin{aligned} i\text{ régulière}&\Longleftrightarrow \forall i\geq1,\ \operatorname{Tor}_i^{A_0}(B_0,A)=0;\\ (1.7.2)\quad i\circ j\text{ régulière}&\Longleftrightarrow \forall i\geq1,\ \operatorname{Tor}_i^{A_0}(C_0,A)=0;\\ j\text{ régulière}&\Longleftrightarrow \forall i\geq1,\ \operatorname{Tor}_i^{B_0}(C_0,B)=0. \end{aligned}\]
We then use the transitivity spectral sequence ([1], XVI 5)
\[(1.7.3)\qquad E^2_{pq}=\operatorname{Tor}_p^{B_0}\bigl(C_0,\operatorname{Tor}_q^{A_0}(B_0,A)\bigr)\Longrightarrow \operatorname{Tor}_n^{A_0}(C_0,A).\]
By the hypotheses of i), \(E^2_{pq}=0\) for \(p\) or \(q\neq0\). Hence it degenerates, and \(i\circ j\) is regular by (1.7.2).
By (1.6.1), the sequence:
\[j^*(N_{X/Y})\longrightarrow N_{X/Z}\longrightarrow N_{Y/Z}\longrightarrow0\]
is exact. Since \(i\circ j\) is regular, the three conormal sheaves are locally free of finite type; moreover, their rank at a point is the
number of elements of a minimal system of generators of the corresponding ideal; by the preceding proof, we have
\[\operatorname{rang}(N_{X/Z})=\operatorname{rang}(N_{X/Y})+\operatorname{rang}(N_{Y/Z}).\]
It follows that the kernel of \(j^*(N_{X/Y})\longrightarrow N_{X/Z}\) is locally free of rank zero, hence that the sequence (1.8.1) is exact.

ii) We may assume \(J/J^2\) free over \(C\), and \(I/I^2\) free over \(B\); moreover, the hypothesis made on the sequence of conormal sheaves allows us to assume that \(I/I^2\otimes_A A/J\) is a direct factor of \(J/J^2\). We can therefore find elements \(f_1,\ldots,f_n\) of \(A\) lifting a basis of \(I/I^2\), and elements \(g_1,\ldots,g_m\) of \(A\) such that \(f_1,\ldots,f_n,g_1,\ldots,g_m\) lift a basis of \(J/J^2\), the images of \(g_1,\ldots,g_m\) generating \(K/K^2\); localizing again if necessary, we may assume that \(f_1,\ldots,f_n\) form a minimal system of generators of \(I\), that \(f_1,\ldots,f_n,g_1,\ldots,g_m\) form a minimal system of generators of \(J\), and that \(\bar g_1,\ldots,\bar g_m\) generate \(K\). We can then construct the diagram (1.7.1), and use the spectral sequence (1.7.3).

By (1.7.2), \(i\) being regular, we have \(E^2_{pq}=0\) for \(q\geq1\); we thus obtain edge-isomorphisms: \(E^2_{p,0}\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\operatorname{Tor}_p^{A_0}(C_0,A)\). Since \(i\circ j\) is regular, this implies by (1.7.2) that \(E^2_{p,0}=0\) for \(p\geq1\), and consequently that \(j\) is regular, again by (1.7.2).
iii) This last property is proved in EGA IV 19.1.5 (iv), since in the noetherian case the definition of EGA IV coincides with the one we adopt here.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.8.

Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(E\) a locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module of finite type, \(u:E\longrightarrow\mathcal O_X\) a regular homomorphism, \(J=u(E)\). We denote by \(Y\)

the closed subscheme of \(X\) defined by \(J\), by \(X'\) the scheme obtained by blowing up \(Y\), by \(P\) the projective bundle associated to \(E\), by \(p\) the structural morphism \(P\longrightarrow X\). On \(P\), we consider the canonical exact sequence:

\[0\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow p^*(E)\longrightarrow\mathcal O_P(1)\longrightarrow0,\]

and the composed morphism:

\[v:F\longrightarrow p^*(E)\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{p^*(u)}}\mathcal O_P.\]

Then:

i) The formation of \(X'\) commutes with every base change \(f:T\longrightarrow X\) such that \(f^*(u)\) is regular.

ii) The closed immersion \(j:X'\longrightarrow P\) defined by the surjective homomorphism \(S(E)\longrightarrow\bigoplus_{n\geq0}J^n\) defined by \(u\) in degree 1 is a regular immersion, its ideal being the image of the regular morphism \(v\); in particular if \(\operatorname{codim}(i)\) is constant, \(\operatorname{codim}(j)=\operatorname{codim}(i)-1\).

iii) The \(S(E)\)-Module \(\bigoplus_{n\geq0}J^n\) is perfect as a graded \(S(E)\)-Module, i.e. there exists locally a finite, graded resolution of \(\bigoplus_{n\geq0}J^n\) by graded \(S(E)\)-Modules which are locally free of finite type (VI 1.2).
i) Since \(X'=\operatorname{Proj}(\bigoplus_{n\geq0}J^n)\), the result is an immediate consequence of the relations (1.2.3) and of EGA II.
ii) The properties stated being local on \(X\), we may assume \(X\) affine, and moreover \(E\) free over \(X\). We set \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\).

We first carry out the proof in the standard case \(A=\mathbf Z[T_1,\ldots,T_d]\), \(E\) being equipped with a basis \(e_1,\ldots,e_d\), with \(u(e_i)=T_i\). Then \(P=\operatorname{Proj}(\mathbf Z[T_1,\ldots,T_d,X_1,\ldots,X_d])\), the grading being taken with respect to the \(X_i\). To show that \(v\) is a regular morphism whose image is the ideal of \(j\), it suffices to do so locally on \(P\), hence on the

\(D^+(X_i)\); let us place ourselves for example on \(D^+(X_1)\). Then \(p^*(E)\) is defined by the module \(\mathbf Z[T_1,\ldots,T_d,X_2/X_1,\ldots,X_d/X_1]\otimes E\), and \(\mathcal O_P(1)\) by \(\mathbf Z[T_1,\ldots,T_d,X_2/X_1,\ldots,X_d/X_1]\), the canonical morphism sending \(e_i\) to \(X_i/X_1\) for every \(i\). Then \(F\) admits as a basis the elements \(e_i-e_1X_i/X_1\) for \(i>1\).

We have \(v(e_i-e_1X_i/X_1)=T_i-T_1X_i/X_1\); let us set \(T'_i=T_i-T_1X_i/X_1\). Since the sequence of the \(T_i\) is regular in \(\mathbf Z[T_1,\ldots,T_d,X_2/X_1,\ldots,X_d/X_1]\), the same holds for the sequence \(T_1,T'_2,\ldots,T'_d\) which is deduced from it by an invertible matrix, and hence also for the sequence \(T'_2,\ldots,T'_d\) which is extracted from it (we are in a noetherian case). Consequently, \(v\) is a regular morphism.

We must therefore show that the \(T'_i\) generate the ideal of \(j\). In \(\bigoplus_{n\geq0}J^n\), we will denote, for \(m\leq p\), by \(x^{(m)}\) the element \(x\) of \(J^p\), regarded as being of degree \(m\). Above \(D^+(X_1)\), \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}((\bigoplus_{n\geq0}J^n)_{(T_1^{(1)})})\), and \(j\) is defined by the morphism which sends \(X_i\) to \(T_i^{(1)}\). In this morphism, the image of \(T'_i\) is \(T_i^{(0)}-T_1^{(0)}T_i^{(1)}/T_1^{(1)}=((T_iT_1)^{(1)}-(T_1T_i)^{(1)})/T_1^{(1)}=0\). Hence the \(T'_i\) belong to the ideal of \(j\).

Since every element of \(\mathbf Z[T_1,\ldots,T_d,X_2/X_1,\ldots,X_d/X_1]\) can be expressed as a polynomial with respect to \(T_1,T'_2,\ldots,T'_d,X_2/X_1,\ldots,X_d/X_1\), it suffices, to show that the \(T'_i\) generate the ideal of \(j\), to show that no nonzero element of \(\mathbf Z[T_1,X_1,\ldots,X_d]\) belongs to the ideal of \(j\), which amounts to showing that the homomorphism
\[\mathbf Z[T_1,X_1,\ldots,X_d]\longrightarrow\bigoplus_{n\geq0}J^n\]

is injective, since \(T_1^{(1)}\) is not a zero divisor in \(\bigoplus_{n\geq0}J^n\). If we grade \(\mathbf Z[T_1,X_1,\ldots,X_d]\) by assigning degree 0 to \(T_1\), and degree 1 to the \(X_i\), the kernel of the homomorphism considered is a graded ideal. Now it is clear that
this kernel is contained in that of the composed homomorphism:
\[\mathbf Z[T_1,X_1,\ldots,X_d]\longrightarrow\bigoplus_{n\geq0}J^n\longrightarrow A,\]

the last one being defined in degree \(n\) by the injection of \(J^n\) into \(A\). Now the kernel of the composed homomorphism is the principal ideal generated by \((X_1-T_1)\), which for reasons of integrality contains no nonzero homogeneous elements. Hence the first homomorphism is injective, which completes the proof in the case considered.

Let us return to the general case where \(A\) is arbitrary; we may again assume \(E\) free over \(X\). Let us assign the index 0 to the particular case which has just been treated; then by part i) of the proposition, the situation considered is obtained by the base change \(A_0\longrightarrow A\) from the situation studied previously, the homomorphism \(A_0\longrightarrow A\) being defined by taking a basis of \(E\), its image \(f_1,\ldots,f_d\) in \(J\), and sending \(T_i\) to \(f_i\). Let us set \(B_0=(\bigoplus_{n\geq0}J_0^n)_{(T_1^{(1)})}\), \(B=(\bigoplus_{n\geq0}J^n)_{(f_1^{(1)})}\); then \(B=B_0\otimes_{A_0}A\); the Koszul complex defined by \(v:F\longrightarrow\mathcal O_P\) is deduced by base change from the Koszul complex defined by \(v_0\); the latter being a projective resolution of \(B_0\) (placing ourselves on a suitable open set), the homology groups of \(K_\bullet(v)\) are on this open set the \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^{A_0}(A,B_0)\). The latter are the degree 0 components of the \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^{A_0}(A,(\bigoplus_{n\geq0}J_0^n)_{T_1^{(1)}})=(\bigoplus_{n\geq0}\operatorname{Tor}_i^{A_0}(A,J_0^n))_{T_1^{(1)}}\); now the \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^{A_0}(A,J_0^n)\) are zero for \(i\geq1\) by the relations 1.2, hence \(K_\bullet(v)\) is a resolution of \(B\), which shows that \(j\) is regular.
iii) The property being local on \(X\), we may again assume \(X\) affine and \(E\) free of finite type. We then take up again the notations of ii).
We first consider the standard case of ii). The ring \(A_0\) is then noetherian and of finite cohomological dimension, and the algebra \(S(E_0)\) like-

wise. We can then construct step by step a graded partial resolution of \(\bigoplus_{n\geq0}J_0^n\), by free graded \(S(E_0)\)-modules of finite type, of length greater than the cohomological dimension of \(A_0\); the last kernel is then a graded \(S(E_0)\)-module, projective of finite type as a non-graded \(S(E_0)\)-module, hence locally free of finite type over \(\operatorname{Spec}(A_0)\) as a graded \(S(E_0)\)-module by VI 1.5.1. We then obtain the announced finite resolution by replacing the last module of the partial resolution by the corresponding kernel.

If we tensor the resolution thus constructed with \(A\), we then obtain a graded complex of graded \(S(E)\)-modules which are locally free of finite type, and it is a matter of seeing that it is a resolution of \(\bigoplus_{n\geq0}J^n\). Now the i-th homology Module of the complex thus obtained is a graded \(S(E)\)-Module whose n-th component is \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^{A_0}(A,J_0^n)\), which is zero for \(i\geq1\) by (1.2.4). Consequently, by (1.2.3), we have thus constructed a resolution of the desired type.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.9.

Let \(i:Y\longrightarrow X\) be a regular closed immersion, \(X'\) the \(X\)-scheme obtained by blowing up \(Y\), and \(f:X'\longrightarrow X\) the structural morphism. Then \(i\) and \(f\) are perfect morphisms. (III 4.1.).

The property being local on \(X\), we may assume given a locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module of finite type \(E\), and a regular homomorphism \(u:E\longrightarrow\mathcal O_X\), having as image the ideal \(J\) of \(Y\). The Koszul complex \(K_\bullet(u)\) is then a finite resolution of \(\mathcal O_Y\) by locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-Modules of finite type; consequently, \(\mathcal O_Y\) is a perfect \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module. It follows that every regular immersion is a perfect morphism. By 1.8, \(f\) can be written as the composite of a regular immersion \(X'\longrightarrow P(E)\) and the structural morphism \(P(E)\longrightarrow X\) which is smooth, hence perfect; it is therefore
the composite of two perfect morphisms, and consequently a perfect morphism.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.10.

Let \(X,Y\) be two schemes smooth over \(S\). Then every \(S\)-immersion of \(X\) into \(Y\) is regular. In particular, every section of a smooth morphism is a regular immersion.
The proposition follows immediately from EGA IV 17.12.1, since an immersion which is regular in the sense of EGA IV is regular in the sense of 1.4.
2. Calculations on regular immersions

2.1. Let \(A\) be a ring, \(B\) and \(C\) two \(A\)-algebras. We propose to equip \(\bigoplus_{i\geq0}\operatorname{Tor}_i^A(B,C)\) with a graded \(A\)-algebra structure, hence to define homomorphisms:
\[(2.1.1)\qquad \operatorname{Tor}_i^A(B,C)\otimes_A\operatorname{Tor}_j^A(B,C)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Tor}_{i+j}^A(B,C).\]

Let \(P_\bullet\) be a resolution of \(B\) by projective \(A\)-modules; the \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^A(B,C)\) are the homology groups of the complex \(P_\bullet\otimes_A C\). We know how to define canonical homomorphisms ([1], IV 6):
\[(2.1.2)\qquad H_i(P_\bullet\otimes_A C)\otimes_A H_j(P_\bullet\otimes_A C)\longrightarrow H_{i+j}((P_\bullet\otimes_A C)\otimes_A(P_\bullet\otimes_A C)).\]

Moreover, \(P_\bullet\otimes_A P_\bullet\) is a complex of projective \(A\)-modules, in degrees \(\geq0\), and \(H_0(P_\bullet\otimes_A P_\bullet)=B\otimes_A B\); furthermore, \(P_\bullet\) is a resolution of \(B\). Consequently, the augmentation morphism \(B\otimes_A B\longrightarrow B\) extends to a morphism of complexes: \(P_\bullet\otimes_A P_\bullet\longrightarrow P_\bullet\) and therefore defines, taking into account the homomorphism \(C\otimes_A C\longrightarrow C\) deduced from the algebra structure of \(C\), homomorphisms:
\[(2.1.3)\qquad H_{i+j}((P_\bullet\otimes_A C)\otimes_A(P_\bullet\otimes_A C))\longrightarrow H_{i+j}(P_\bullet\otimes_A C).\]
By composing (2.1.2) and (2.1.3), we obtain the homomorphisms (2.1.1).

The algebra structure defined by the homomorphisms (2.1.1) is associative (cf. [1] XI 2). Moreover, the \(A\)-module \(H_i(P_\bullet\otimes_A C)\) is equipped in the standard way with a \(B\otimes_A C\)-module structure by letting \(C\) act in the obvious way, and \(B\) by functoriality; likewise, the \(H_j((P_\bullet\otimes_A C)\otimes_A(P_\bullet\otimes_A C))\) are equipped with two \(B\otimes_A C\)-module structures. It is clear that the homomorphisms (2.1.2) and (2.1.3) are compatible with these two structures: for (2.1.2), by functoriality (placing ourselves in the category of \(A\)-modules), and for (2.1.3), by using the classical result on morphisms from an acyclic complex. We have therefore equipped \(\bigoplus_{i\geq0}\operatorname{Tor}_i^A(B,C)\) with an associative \(B\otimes_A C\)-algebra structure.
2.2. Suppose that \(B\) admits a resolution by projective \(A\)-modules \(P_\bullet\), such that \(P_\bullet=\bigoplus_{i\geq0}P_i\) is a graded \(A\)-algebra, the differential satisfying:
a) \(P_0\longrightarrow B\) is a ring homomorphism;(1)
b) \(\forall x\in P_i,\ \forall y\in P_j,\ d(xy)=d(x)y+(-1)^i x d(y)\).
Then the "product" maps define commutative diagrams for \(i,j\geq0\):
\[\begin{CD}P_i\otimes_A P_j @>>> (P_{i-1}\otimes_A P_j)\oplus(P_i\otimes_A P_{j-1})\\ @VVV @VVV\\ P_{i+j} @>>> P_{i+j-1},\end{CD}\]

and consequently a homomorphism of complexes \(P_\bullet\otimes_A P_\bullet\longrightarrow P_\bullet\) which extends the augmentation homomorphism: \(B\otimes_A B\longrightarrow B\). This shows that the algebra structure of \(\bigoplus_{i\geq0}\operatorname{Tor}_i^A(B,C)\) can be computed from that of \(P_\bullet\). In particular, if \(P_\bullet\) is an alternating \(A\)-algebra (i.e. such that for every
\(x\in P_\bullet\), we have \(x^2=0\)), then the same holds for \(\bigoplus_{i\geq0}\operatorname{Tor}_i^A(B,C)\).

This last remark applies to the case where \(B\) is the quotient of \(A\) by a regular ideal, since we can then take for \(P_\bullet\) the Koszul complex associated to a regular homomorphism defining the ideal of \(B\).

2.3. The \(B\otimes_A C\)-algebra structure defined in 2.1 on \(\bigoplus_{i\geq0}\operatorname{Tor}_i^A(B,C)\) is functorial in \(B\) and \(C\). We can then globalize the preceding constructions: let \(X\) be a scheme, \(X'\), \(X''\) two \(X\)-schemes; then we can define locally on \(X'\times_X X''\) the morphisms (2.1.1), they glue together by functoriality, and define on \(\bigoplus_{i\geq0}\operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_{X'},\mathcal O_{X''})\) a structure of associative graded \(\mathcal O_{X'\times_X X''}\)-Algebra.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.4.

Let \(X'\), \(X''\) be two \(X\)-schemes, and \(M\) a flat \(\mathcal O_{X'}\)-Module. Then for every \(i\geq0\), we have a canonical isomorphism:

\[\operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(M,\mathcal O_{X''})\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}M\otimes_{\mathcal O_{X'}}\operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_{X'},\mathcal O_{X''}).\]
It suffices to define a functorial isomorphism in the affine case; we can then, by gluing, define it globally in the general case.

So let \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(A')\), \(X''=\operatorname{Spec}(A'')\); \(M\) is defined by a flat \(A'\)-module \(M\). Let \(P_\bullet\) be a projective resolution of \(A''\) over \(A\); the \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^A(A',A'')\) are the homology groups of \(A'\otimes_A P_\bullet\). Since \(M\) is flat over \(A'\), we have a canonical isomorphism: \(M\otimes_{A'}\operatorname{Tor}_i^A(A',A'')\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}H_i(M\otimes_{A'}(A'\otimes_A P_\bullet))\). Moreover, the canonical isomorphism: \(M\otimes_{A'}(A'\otimes_A P_\bullet)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}M\otimes_A P_\bullet\) gives a canonical isomorphism: \(H_i(M\otimes_{A'}(A'\otimes_A P_\bullet))\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\operatorname{Tor}_i^A(M,A'')\), whence the result.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.5.

Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(Y\) and \(Z\) two closed subschemes of \(X\), \(T=Y\times_X Z\). We assume that for every \(x\in T\), there exists an open neighborhood

\(U\) of \(x\), and a sequence of sections \(f_1,\ldots,f_p,g_1,\ldots,g_r,h_1,\ldots,h_s\) of \(\mathcal O_U\) such that the Koszul complex associated to \(f_1,\ldots,f_p,g_1,\ldots,g_r\) is a resolution of \(\mathcal O_{Y\cap U}\), the Koszul complex associated to \(f_1,\ldots,f_p,h_1,\ldots,h_s\) a resolution of \(\mathcal O_{Z\cap U}\), and the Koszul complex associated to \(f_1,\ldots,f_p,g_1,\ldots,g_r,h_1,\ldots,h_s\) a resolution of \(\mathcal O_{T\cap U}\) (conditions satisfied in particular in the noetherian case if the sequence \(f_1,\ldots,f_p,g_1,\ldots,g_r,h_1,\ldots,h_s\) is regular and if \(Y=V(f_1,\ldots,f_p,g_1,\ldots,g_r)\), \(Z=V(f_1,\ldots,f_p,h_1,\ldots,h_s)\)). Let \(I,J\) be the ideals of \(Y,Z\). We then have the following isomorphisms:

i) \(\forall i\geq0,\quad \operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_Y,\mathcal O_Z)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\bigwedge^i\operatorname{Tor}_1^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_Y,\mathcal O_Z)\);

ii) \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_Y,\mathcal O_Z)\simeq I\cap J/IJ\simeq\operatorname{Ker}(i^*(N_{Y/X})\times j^*(N_{Z/X})\longrightarrow N_{T/X})\), where we denote by \(i\) the immersion of \(T\) into \(Y\) and by \(j\) the immersion of \(T\) into \(Z\).

In particular, for every regular closed immersion \(Y\longrightarrow X\), we have

i') \(\forall i\geq0,\quad \operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_Y,\mathcal O_Y)\simeq\bigwedge^i\operatorname{Tor}_1^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_Y,\mathcal O_Y)\);

ii') \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_Y,\mathcal O_Y)\simeq N_{Y/X}\).

i) We saw in 2.3 that \(\bigoplus_{i\geq0}\operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_Y,\mathcal O_Z)\) could be equipped with a \(\mathcal O_T\)-Algebra structure; by 2.2, this algebra is alternating, since the immersion of \(Y\) (or \(Z\)) into \(X\) is regular. There therefore exists one and only one algebra homomorphism:
\[(2.5.1)\qquad \bigwedge\operatorname{Tor}_1^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_Y,\mathcal O_Z)\longrightarrow\bigoplus_{i\geq0}\operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_Y,\mathcal O_Z),\]
extending the identity in degree 1.

To see that it is an isomorphism, we may check it locally. We therefore place ourselves in a neighborhood of a point of \(T\), and consider sections \(f_1,\ldots,f_p,g_1,\ldots,g_r,h_1,\ldots,h_s\) of \(\mathcal O_X\) in this neighborhood, having the properties indicated in the statement of 2.5. Let us then set \(E=(\mathcal O_X)^p\), \(E'=(\mathcal O_X)^r\), and let \(u:E\longrightarrow\mathcal O_X\) be the homomorphism defined by the \(f_i\), \(v:E'\longrightarrow\mathcal O_X\) the homomorphism defined by the \(g_j\), \(K_\bullet(u)\) and \(K_\bullet(v)\) the

associated Koszul complexes. The Koszul complex associated to the sequence \(f_1,\ldots,f_p,g_1,\ldots,g_r\) is isomorphic to the tensor product complex \(K_\bullet(u)\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}K_\bullet(v)\). Since it is a resolution of \(\mathcal O_Y\) by free \(\mathcal O_X\)-Modules of finite type, the \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_Y,\mathcal O_Z)\) are the homology sheaves of the complex \(K_\bullet(u)\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}K_\bullet(v)\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}\mathcal O_Z\). We can regard it as the tensor product of the complexes \(K_\bullet(u)\) and \(K_\bullet(v)\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}\mathcal O_Z\), and use, to compute its homology, the spectral sequences associated to a bicomplex (EGA \(0_{\mathrm{III}}\) (11.3.5)), which have as abutment the homology of the associated simple complex.

We consider the spectral sequence such that \({}'E^2_{pq}=H_p^{\mathrm I}(H_q^{\mathrm{II}}(K_{\bullet\bullet}))\), with the notations of EGA \(0_{\mathrm{III}}\) (11.3.5), the first index being relative to the complex \(K_\bullet(u)\) and the second to the complex \(K_\bullet(v)\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}\mathcal O_Z\). Now the complex \(K_\bullet(v)\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}\mathcal O_Z\) is the Koszul complex on \(\mathcal O_Z\) associated to the sequence of sections \(\bar g_1,\ldots,\bar g_r\), where the \(\bar g_i\) are the images of the \(g_i\) in \(\mathcal O_Z\), and this complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal O_T\) on \(\mathcal O_Z\) since the immersions \(Z\longrightarrow X\), and \(T\longrightarrow Z\longrightarrow X\) are regular (cf. proof of 1.7 ii). Since \(\bigwedge^pE\) is free over \(\mathcal O_X\), the complex \(\bigwedge^pE\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}(K_\bullet(v)\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}\mathcal O_Z)\) is a resolution of \(\bigwedge^pE\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}\mathcal O_T\). Consequently the spectral sequence degenerates, and the \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_Y,\mathcal O_Z)\) are the homology sheaves of the complex \(K_\bullet(u)\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}\mathcal O_T\). Now the differential of \(K_\bullet(u)\) is expressed in terms of the \(f_i\), which annihilate \(\mathcal O_T\); consequently, the differential of \(K_\bullet(u)\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}\mathcal O_T\) is zero, and we therefore obtain the isomorphisms:

\[(2.5.2)\qquad \operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_Y,\mathcal O_Z)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\mathcal O_T\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}\bigwedge^iE\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\bigwedge^i(\mathcal O_T)^p.\]
In particular, we find for \(i=1\):
\[\operatorname{Tor}_1^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_Y,\mathcal O_Z)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}(\mathcal O_T)^p,\]
whence for every \(i\):
\[(2.5.3)\qquad \operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_Y,\mathcal O_Z)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\bigwedge^i\operatorname{Tor}_1^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_Y,\mathcal O_Z).\]
Now it follows from 2.2 that the isomorphism:
\[\bigwedge(\mathcal O_T)^p\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\bigoplus_{i\geq0}\operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_Y,\mathcal O_Z)\]
defined by the isomorphisms (2.5.2) is compatible with the algebra structures of the two sides; the same therefore holds for the isomorphism:
\[\bigwedge\operatorname{Tor}_1^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_Y,\mathcal O_Z)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\bigoplus_{i\geq0}\operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_Y,\mathcal O_Z)\]

defined by the isomorphisms (2.5.3). For reasons of uniqueness, the latter is therefore the restriction of the homomorphism (2.5.1) which had been defined globally. The homomorphism (2.5.1) is therefore an isomorphism.
ii) We consider the exact sequence:
\[0\longrightarrow I\longrightarrow\mathcal O_X\longrightarrow\mathcal O_Y\longrightarrow0,\]
and the exact sequence of Tor's resulting from it:
\[0\longrightarrow\operatorname{Tor}_1^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_Y,\mathcal O_Z)\longrightarrow I\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}\mathcal O_Z\longrightarrow\mathcal O_Z.\]
It follows that \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_Y,\mathcal O_Z)\) is the kernel of the homomorphism:
\[I\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_X/J)\longrightarrow\mathcal O_X/J,\]
that is to say \(I\cap J/IJ\).
To show the last relation, we shall show that there exists an exact sequence:
\[0\longrightarrow I\cap J/IJ\longrightarrow i^*(N_{Y/X})\times j^*(N_{Z/X})\longrightarrow N_{T/X}\longrightarrow0.\]

The homomorphisms \(i^*(N_{Y/X})\longrightarrow N_{T/X}\) and \(j^*(N_{Z/X})\longrightarrow N_{T/X}\) were defined in (1.6.1); here we take their difference. Moreover, \(I\cap J\) maps naturally into \(I/I^2\), hence into \(i^*(I/I^2)\), and likewise into \(j^*(J/J^2)\), and these homomorphisms vanish on \(IJ\), since \(I,J\subset K\) (where \(K\) denotes the ideal of \(T\)), which defines the left-hand arrow.

To verify that the sequence is exact, we may do so locally on \(T\), and use the sections defined in the statement of 2.5. We then have a basis of \(N_{T/X}\) formed by the images of the \(f_i,g_j,h_k\), a basis of \(i^*(N_{Y/X})\) formed by the images of the \(f_i,g_j\), and a basis of \(j^*(N_{Z/X})\) formed by the images of the \(f_i,h_k\). It is then clear that \(i^*(N_{Y/X})\times j^*(N_{Z/X})\longrightarrow N_{T/X}\) is surjective, and that its kernel is formed of the elements having zero coordinates on the \((\bar g_j,0)\) and the \((0,\bar h_k)\), and equal coordinates for every \(i\) on \((\bar f_i,0)\) and \((0,\bar f_i)\). Consequently, the sequence is also exact in the middle. Now the three \(\mathcal O_T\)-Modules involved are locally free of finite type; since \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_Y,\mathcal O_Z)\) is of rank \(p\) by the proof of i), we see immediately that the sequence is exact.

2.6. We now assume that \(X\) is a scheme having an ample sheaf. Every closed subscheme \(Y\) of \(X\) then has an ample sheaf. We know that in this case "global resolutions exist", and that the \(K'\) formed respectively with the locally free sheaves of finite type and with the perfect complexes are identical (II, 2.2.9). If moreover the immersion \(i:Y\longrightarrow X\) is regular, it is a perfect morphism, and one can define an additive homomorphism \(i_*:K'(Y)\longrightarrow K'(X)\) (IV 2.12), by associating to the class of a perfect complex \(L^\bullet\) the class of the complex \(Ri_*(L^\bullet)=i_*(L^\bullet)\) (\(i_*\) being an exact functor).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.7.

Let \(X\) be a scheme having an ample sheaf, \(i:Y\longrightarrow X\) a regular closed immersion, \(N\) the conormal sheaf of \(Y\) in \(X\). Then, for every \(x\in K'(Y)\), we have the relation:

\[i^*(i_*(x))=x.\lambda_{-1}(N),\]

where \(N\) is the class of \(N\) in \(K'(Y)\), and \(\lambda_{-1}(N)=\sum_{i\geq0}(-1)^i\lambda^i(N)\).

Since the two sides of the relation to be proved are additive, we may assume that \(x\) is the class of a locally free \(\mathcal O_Y\)-Module of finite type \(M\). The element \(i^*(i_*(x))\) is then the class of the complex \(Li^*(Ri_*(M))\), obtained by taking a projective resolution of the complex \(Ri_*(M)=i_*(M)\), and tensoring it with \(\mathcal O_Y\). The homology sheaves of this complex are therefore the \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(M,\mathcal O_Y)\). By 2.4, we have the isomorphism:
\[\operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(M,\mathcal O_Y)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}M\otimes_{\mathcal O_Y}\operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_Y,\mathcal O_Y).\]
By 2.5, we have the isomorphism:
\[\operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_Y,\mathcal O_Y)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\bigwedge^iN.\]

The \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(M,\mathcal O_Y)\) are therefore locally free \(\mathcal O_Y\)-Modules of finite type, and the class in \(K'(Y)\) of the complex \(Li^*(Ri_*(M))\) is the alternating sum of the classes of its homology sheaves, namely:
\[i^*(i_*(x))=\sum_{i\geq0}(-1)^i\operatorname{cl}\!\left(M\otimes_{\mathcal O_Y}\operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_Y,\mathcal O_Y)\right)=\sum_{i\geq0}(-1)^i x.\lambda^i(N).\]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.8.

Under the hypotheses of 2.7, let \(x,y\in K'(Y)\); we have the relation:

\[i_*(xy\lambda_{-1}(N))=i_*(x).i_*(y).\]
By applying the projection formula, we therefore find:
\[i_*(xy\lambda_{-1}(N))=i_*(x.i^*(i_*(y)))=i_*(x).i_*(y).\]

footnotes.html: One can show that such a resolution always exists.

pages.title: 3. Computation of the \(K'\) of a blown-up scheme

blocks.html: 3. Computation of the \(K'\) of a blown-up scheme
3.1. In this section, we denote by \(i:Y\longrightarrow X\) a regular closed immersion. We denote by \(X'\) the scheme obtained by blowing up \(Y\), and \(Y'=Y\times_X X'\).

We know (EGA II 8.1.8) that \(Y'\) is a closed subscheme of \(X'\) defined by an ideal of \(\mathcal O_{X'}\) canonically isomorphic to \(\mathcal O_{X'}(1)\); the immersion \(j:Y'\longrightarrow X'\), being defined by an invertible ideal, is a regular closed immersion of codimension 1. We denote by \(J\) the ideal of \(\mathcal O_X\) which defines \(Y\), and by \(J'\) the ideal of \(\mathcal O_{X'}\) which defines \(Y'\); we thus have \(J'=J.\mathcal O_{X'}\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\mathcal O_{X'}(1)\). Finally, we denote by \(f:X'\longrightarrow X\), and \(g:Y'\longrightarrow Y\) the structural morphisms. We thus have the commutative diagram:
\[(3.1.1)\qquad \begin{CD}Y' @>{j}>> X'\\ @V{g}VV @VV{f}V\\ Y @>{i}>> X.\end{CD}\]
By definition, \(X'=\operatorname{Proj}(S)\), where \(S=\bigoplus_{n\geq0}J^n\). Consequently, we have:
\[Y'=\operatorname{Proj}(i^*(S))=\operatorname{Proj}\!\left((\bigoplus_{n\geq0}J^n)\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_X/J)\right)=\operatorname{Proj}(\bigoplus_{n\geq0}J^n/J^{n+1}).\]

Now, \(i\) being regular, \(\bigoplus_{n\geq0}J^n/J^{n+1}\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\operatorname{Sym}_{\mathcal O_Y}(J/J^2)\) (1.3). We denote by \(N\) the conormal sheaf \(J/J^2\); we thus have:
\[Y'\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\mathbf P(N).\]
On \(Y'\), we can write the exact sequence:
\[(3.1.2)\qquad 0\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow g^*(N)\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{Y'}(1)\longrightarrow0.\]
We remark that \(\mathcal O_{Y'}(1)=j^*(\mathcal O_{X'}(1))=J'/J'^2\); it is therefore the conormal sheaf of the immersion \(j\), and we will denote it by \(L\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.2.

With the hypotheses and notations of 3.1, we have the following isomorphisms:

i) \(\forall i\geq0,\quad \operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_{X'},\mathcal O_Y)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\bigwedge^i\operatorname{Tor}_1^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_{X'},\mathcal O_Y)\) ;

ii) \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_{X'},\mathcal O_Y)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}F\).

i) Since \(\mathcal O_Y\) can be resolved locally by a Koszul complex, the graded algebra \(\bigoplus_{i\geq0}\operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_{X'},\mathcal O_Y)\) is alternating (2.2). There therefore exists one and only one algebra homomorphism:
\[(3.2.1)\qquad \bigwedge \operatorname{Tor}_1^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_{X'},\mathcal O_Y)\longrightarrow\bigoplus_{i\geq0}\operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_{X'},\mathcal O_Y),\]

extending the injection of \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_{X'},\mathcal O_Y)\) into \(\bigoplus_{i\geq0}\operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_{X'},\mathcal O_Y)\). It suffices to verify locally that it is an isomorphism.

We may therefore place ourselves on an affine open \(U=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) of \(X\), \(Y\) being defined by an ideal \(J\) of \(A\), and give ourselves a regular homomorphism \(u:E\longrightarrow A\), defining \(J\), with \(E=(A)^d\). Let \(f_1,\ldots,f_d\) be the images of a basis of \(E\).

In \(\bigoplus_{n\geq0}J^n\), we will denote, for \(m\leq p\), by \(x^{(m)}\) the element \(x\) of \(J^p\) regarded as being of degree \(m\). We may restrict to the open set \(V\) of \(X'\) where the element \(f_1^{(1)}\), for example, is invertible.

The \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_{X'},\mathcal O_Y)\) are the homology sheaves of the complex \(K_\bullet(u)\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}\mathcal O_{X'}\). To study this complex on \(V\), we make the following change of basis in \(E'=E\otimes_A\Gamma(V,\mathcal O_{X'})\): if \(e_1,\ldots,e_d\) is the given basis of \(E\), we set:
\[e'_1=e_1\ ;\qquad \text{pour }i\ne1,\quad e'_i=e_i-(f_i^{(1)}/f_1^{(1)})e_1.\]
Set \(A'=\Gamma(V,\mathcal O_{X'})\); the differential of the complex \(K_\bullet(u)\otimes_AA'\) is given by
\[d(e'_1)=f_1^{(0)}\ ;\qquad \text{pour }i\ne1,\quad d(e'_i)=0.\]
Taking as basis of \(\bigwedge^k(E')\) the elements \(e'_{i_1}\wedge\cdots\wedge e'_{i_k}\), with \(i_1<\cdots<i_k\), we therefore find:

\[ \begin{aligned} d(a.e'_{i_1}\wedge\cdots\wedge e'_{i_k})&=0 &&\text{si }i_1\ne1\ ;\\ d(a.e'_{i_1}\wedge\cdots\wedge e'_{i_k})&=af_1^{(0)}.e'_{i_2}\wedge\cdots\wedge e'_{i_k} &&\text{si }i_1=1. \end{aligned}\]
Denoting by \(E''\) the submodule of \(E'\) generated by \(e'_2,\ldots,e'_d\), we thus see that the image of \(d:\bigwedge^kE'\longrightarrow\bigwedge^{k-1}E'\) is \(f_1^{(0)}\bigwedge^{k-1}E''\).

Moreover, \(f_1^{(0)}\) is regular in \(A'\), for on \(V\) the invertible ideal \(J'\) is defined by the equation \(f_1^{(0)}\). The kernel of \(d:\bigwedge^kE'\longrightarrow\bigwedge^{k-1}E'\) is therefore \(\bigwedge^kE''\). Consequently, the \(k\)-th homology group of \(K_\bullet(u)\otimes_AA'\) is \(\bigwedge^kE''/f_1^{(0)}\bigwedge^kE''\); we thus find on \(V\) isomorphisms:

\[(3.2.2)\qquad \operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_{X'},\mathcal O_Y) \mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow} (\mathcal O_{X'}/f_1^{(0)}\mathcal O_{X'})\otimes_{\mathcal O_{X'}}\bigwedge^i(\mathcal O_{X'})^{d-1} \mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\bigwedge^i(\mathcal O_{Y'})^{d-1}.\]
In particular, we find for \(i=1\):
\[\operatorname{Tor}_1^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_{X'},\mathcal O_Y)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}(\mathcal O_{Y'})^{d-1}.\]
Consequently, we have for every \(i\):
\[(3.2.3)\qquad \operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_{X'},\mathcal O_Y)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\bigwedge^i\operatorname{Tor}_1^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_{X'},\mathcal O_Y).\]
Now by 2.2, the isomorphism:
\[\bigwedge(\mathcal O_{Y'})^{d-1}\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\bigoplus_{i\geq0}\operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_{X'},\mathcal O_Y)\]
defined by the isomorphisms (3.2.2) is an algebra isomorphism. The same therefore holds for the isomorphism:
\[\bigwedge\operatorname{Tor}_1^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_{X'},\mathcal O_Y)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\bigoplus_{i\geq0}\operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_{X'},\mathcal O_Y)\]
defined by the isomorphisms (3.2.3). For reasons of uniqueness, it is then the restriction to \(V\) of the homomorphism (3.2.1), and the latter is therefore an isomorphism.
ii) We consider the exact sequence:
\[0\longrightarrow J\longrightarrow\mathcal O_X\longrightarrow\mathcal O_Y\longrightarrow0,\]
and the exact sequence of Tor's obtained by tensoring with \(\mathcal O_{X'}\):

\[0\longrightarrow\operatorname{Tor}_1^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_{X'},\mathcal O_Y)\longrightarrow J\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}\mathcal O_{X'}\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{X'}\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{Y'}\longrightarrow0.\]
The image of the morphism: \(J\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}\mathcal O_{X'}\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{X'}\) is \(J.\mathcal O_{X'}=\mathcal O_{X'}(1)\). Consequently, we obtain the exact sequence:
\[0\longrightarrow\operatorname{Tor}_1^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_{X'},\mathcal O_Y)\longrightarrow J\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}\mathcal O_{X'}\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{X'}(1)\longrightarrow0.\]

We then take its tensor product with \(\mathcal O_{Y'}\); since \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_{X'},\mathcal O_Y)\) is a \(\mathcal O_{Y'}\)-Module, and \(\mathcal O_{X'}(1)\) is invertible, we obtain the exact sequence:
\[0\longrightarrow\operatorname{Tor}_1^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_{X'},\mathcal O_Y)\longrightarrow g^*(N)\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{Y'}(1)\longrightarrow0.\]

One easily verifies that the morphism \(g^*(N)\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{Y'}(1)\) appearing in this sequence is the canonical morphism, so that it is identified with the sequence (3.1.2). By definition, we thus have:
\[\operatorname{Tor}_1^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_{X'},\mathcal O_Y)=F.\]

3.3. We now assume that \(X\) is a scheme having an ample sheaf. Then the same holds for \(X'\), which is projective over \(X\), and for \(Y\) and \(Y'\). Moreover, \(i\) and \(j\) are perfect morphisms, since they are regular immersions; \(g\) is a smooth morphism, hence perfect, since \(Y'=\mathbf P(N)\); finally, \(f\) is perfect by 1.9. These morphisms being projective, we can define the additive homomorphisms (IV
\[ \begin{aligned} i_*:K'(Y)&\longrightarrow K'(X)\ ;& j_*:K'(Y')&\longrightarrow K'(X')\ ;\\ f_*:K'(X')&\longrightarrow K'(X)\ ;& g_*:K'(Y')&\longrightarrow K'(Y). \end{aligned}\]

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.4.

With the hypotheses and notations of 3.1 and 3.3, we have for every \(x\in K'(Y)\) the relation:

\[f^*(i_*(x))=j_*(g^*(x).\lambda_{-1}(F)).\]

Since the two sides of the relation to be proved are additive, we may assume that \(x\) is the class of a locally free \(\mathcal O_Y\)-Module of finite type \(M\). The element \(f^*(i_*(x))\) is then the class of the complex \(Lf^*(Ri_*(M))\), obtained by taking a projective resolution of \(i_*(M)\) and applying to it the functor \(f^*\). The homology sheaves of the complex thus obtained are the \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(M,\mathcal O_{X'})\). Now by 2.4 and 3.2, we have:

\[\operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(M,\mathcal O_{X'})\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}M\otimes_{\mathcal O_Y}\operatorname{Tor}_i^{\mathcal O_X}(\mathcal O_{X'},\mathcal O_Y)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}M\otimes_{\mathcal O_Y}\bigwedge^iF.\]

It follows that these homology sheaves are locally free of finite type on \(\mathcal O_{Y'}\), hence perfect on \(\mathcal O_{X'}\); consequently, the class in \(K'(X')\) of the complex \(Lf^*(Ri_*(M))\) is the alternating sum of the classes of its homology sheaves, namely:
\[f^*(i_*(x))=\sum_{i\geq0}(-1)^i\operatorname{cl}\!\left(j_*\left(M\otimes_{\mathcal O_Y}\bigwedge^iF\right)\right)=\sum_{i\geq0}(-1)^ij_*(g^*(x)\lambda^i(F)).\]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.5.

With the hypotheses and notations of 3.1, we have the following isomorphisms:

i) for every \(n\geq0\), and for every \(i\geq1\), \(R^if_*(\mathcal O_{X'}(n))=0\) ;

ii) for every \(n\geq0\), \(f_*(\mathcal O_{X'}(n))=J^n\).
We may obviously assume \(X\) affine, hence quasi-compact.
i) Let us first recall the following lemma, which was proved in exp.

blocks.title: Lemma.
blocks.html:
Lemma.

Let \(X'\xrightarrow{f}X\) be a projective and perfect morphism, with \(X\) quasi-compact, and let \(L^\bullet\) be a perfect complex on \(\mathcal O_{X'}\), acyclic in degrees \(>0\). Then there exists an integer \(n_0\) such that for \(n\geq n_0\) the complex \(Rf_*(L^\bullet\otimes_{\mathcal O_{X'}}\mathcal O_{X'}(n))\) is acyclic in degrees \(>0\).

We apply this lemma to the complex reduced to \(\mathcal O_{X'}\); there thus exists an integer \(n_0\) such that for \(n\geq n_0\), we have \(R^if_*(\mathcal O_{X'}(n))=0\) if \(i\geq1\). We then descend by induction down to \(n=0\). To do this, we consider the exact sequence:
\[0\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{X'}(n+1)\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{X'}(n)\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{Y'}(n)\longrightarrow0,\]

and we apply to it the functor \(f_*\). Now we have \(Rf_*(\mathcal O_{Y'}(n))=i_*Rg_*(\mathcal O_{Y'}(n))\). Since \(Y'=\mathbf P(N)\), \(\mathcal O_{Y'}(n)\) is \(g_*\)-acyclic for \(n\geq0\). We thus have for every \(n\geq0\), and every \(i\geq1\):
\[R^if_*(\mathcal O_{Y'}(n))=0,\]
and consequently the exact sequences:
\[R^if_*(\mathcal O_{X'}(n+1))\longrightarrow R^if_*(\mathcal O_{X'}(n))\longrightarrow0,\]
which allows us to complete the induction.
ii) It is a matter of showing that the canonical morphism:
\[\bigoplus_{n\geq0}J^n\longrightarrow\bigoplus_{n\geq0}f_*(\mathcal O_{X'}(n))\]

is an isomorphism. The property being local, we may assume \(J\) defined by a sequence \(f_1,\ldots,f_d\) of sections of \(\mathcal O_X\), such that the corresponding homomorphism \((\mathcal O_X)^d\longrightarrow\mathcal O_X\) is regular. Set: \(S=\bigoplus_{n\geq0}J^n\); we define a surjective algebra homomorphism: \(\mathcal O_X[T_1,\ldots,T_d]\xrightarrow{v}S\), by sending \(T_i\) to \(f_i^{(1)}\). This homomorphism defines a closed immersion \(X'\longrightarrow\mathbf P_X^{d-1}\), which is regular by 1.8 ii).
By 1.8 iii), \(S\) is perfect as a graded \(\mathcal O_X[T_1,\ldots,T_d]\)-Module. In particular, \(S\) is of finite presentation as a graded Module. Let then:
\[L''\longrightarrow L'\longrightarrow S\longrightarrow0\]
be a finite graded presentation of \(S\) over \(\mathcal O_X[T_1,\ldots,T_d]\). We obtain on \(\mathbf P_X^{d-1}\) the exact sequence:
\[M''\longrightarrow M'\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{X'}\longrightarrow0,\]

where \(M''=\operatorname{Proj}(L'')\), \(M'=\operatorname{Proj}(L')\), and these are therefore free of finite type over \(\mathbf P_X^{d-1}\). We can therefore write for every \(n\) the commutative diagram:

\[ \begin{array}{ccccc} L''_n&\longrightarrow&L'_n&\longrightarrow&J^n\longrightarrow0\\ \downarrow\wr&&\downarrow\wr&&\downarrow\\ p_*(M''(n))&\longrightarrow&p_*(M'(n))&\longrightarrow&p_*(\mathcal O_{X'}(n))\longrightarrow0, \end{array} \]

where \(p\) is the structural morphism: \(\mathbf P_X^{d-1}\longrightarrow X\), and \(L''_n\), \(L'_n\) the degree-\(n\) components of \(L'',L'\). The top row is exact by hypothesis; the first two vertical arrows are isomorphisms because of the classical results for direct images in \(\mathcal O_P(n)\), \(P\) being a projective bundle (EGA III, 2.1.15); finally, the last row is exact if \(n\) is large enough, for the kernel of \(M'\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{X'}\) is a perfect \(\mathcal O_P\)-Module, and we can apply the lemma recalled above, which shows that this kernel, tensored with \(\mathcal O_P(n)\) for \(n\) large enough, is \(p_*\)-acyclic; likewise for the kernel of \(M''\longrightarrow M'\). It then follows that for \(n\) large enough, \(J^n\longrightarrow p_*(\mathcal O_{X'}(n))\) is an isomorphism. Now \(p_*(\mathcal O_{X'}(n))=f_*(\mathcal O_{X'}(n))\); we thus have
hence the result for \(n\geq n_0\).
It remains to descend down to \(n=0\). We proceed by descending induction, thanks to the commutative diagram:

\[ \begin{array}{ccccccccc} 0&\longrightarrow&J^{n+1}&\longrightarrow&J^n&\longrightarrow&J^n/J^{n+1}&\longrightarrow&0\\ \downarrow&&\downarrow\wr&&\downarrow&&\downarrow\wr&&\downarrow\\ 0&\longrightarrow&f_*(\mathcal O_{X'}(n+1))&\longrightarrow&f_*(\mathcal O_{X'}(n))&\longrightarrow&f_*(\mathcal O_{Y'}(n))&\longrightarrow&0. \end{array} \]
The bottom row is exact since \(R^1f_*(\mathcal O_{X'}(n+1))=0\) for every \(n\geq0\) by i) already proved. Moreover, we have:
\[f_*(\mathcal O_{Y'}(n))=i_*\bigl(g_*(\mathcal O_{Y'}(n))\bigr)=i_*(S(N)_n)=J^n/J^{n+1}.\]
The right-hand arrow is therefore an isomorphism; the left-hand one is one by the induction hypothesis; the middle one is therefore one as well.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.6.

With the hypotheses and notations of 3.1 and 3.3, we have the relation:

\[f_*\circ f^*=\operatorname{Id}_{K'(X)}.\]
Let \(x\in K'(X)\); we can write, by the projection formula:
\[f_*(f^*(x))=f_*(1.f^*(x))=x.f_*(1).\]
It therefore suffices to show that \(f_*(1)=1\).
Now 3.5 shows that the cohomology of \(Rf_*(\mathcal O_{X'})\) is zero in degrees \(>0\), and equal to \(\mathcal O_X\) in degree 0. The proposition follows immediately.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.7.

Let \(i:Y\longrightarrow X\) be a regular closed immersion, \(X'\) the scheme obtained by blowing up \(Y\), and \(Y'=i^*X'\), so that we have the commutative diagram

\[ \begin{array}{ccc} Y'&\xrightarrow{j}&X'\\ \downarrow g&&\downarrow f\\ Y&\xrightarrow{i}&X. \end{array} \]

We assume that \(X\) is quasi-compact and has an ample sheaf. Then we have the exact sequence:

\[0\longrightarrow K'(Y)\xrightarrow{u}K'(Y')\times K'(X)\xrightarrow{v}K'(X'),\]

where we set, for \(y\in K'(Y)\):

\[u(y)=\bigl(g^*(y).\lambda_{-1}(F),-i_*(y)\bigr),\]

and for \(y'\in K'(Y')\), \(x\in K'(X)\):

\[v(y',x)=j_*(y')+f^*(x),\]

the notations being those of 3.1 and 3.3. Moreover, \(u\) admits a left inverse \(u'\), defined by \(u'(y',x)=g_*(y')\).

If in addition \(X\) is noetherian, and \(Y\) regular, then \(v\) is surjective, so we have the exact sequence:

\[0\longrightarrow K'(Y)\xrightarrow{u}K'(Y')\times K'(X)\xrightarrow{v}K'(X')\longrightarrow0.\]
First of all, \(u\) admits the left inverse \(u'\), and a fortiori is injective. Indeed, we have:
\[g_*\bigl(g^*(y).\lambda_{-1}(F)\bigr)=y.g_*(\lambda_{-1}(F))\]
by the projection formula. Moreover, \(g_*(\lambda_{-1}(F))=1\) (VI 5.9), whence the result.
Next, \(v\circ u=0\). If \(y\in K'(Y)\), we indeed have:
\[v\circ u(y)=v\bigl(g^*(y)\lambda_{-1}(F),-i_*(y)\bigr)=j_*\bigl(g^*(y)\lambda_{-1}(F)\bigr)-f^*(i_*(y))=0,\]
It therefore remains to show that \(\operatorname{Ker}(v)\subset\operatorname{Im}(u)\). Let then \(x\in K'(X)\), \(y'\in K'(Y')\), be such that:
\[j_*(y')+f^*(x)=0.\]
We must find \(y\in K'(Y)\) such that:
\[(3.7.1)\qquad x=-i_*(y)\quad;\qquad y'=g^*(y).\lambda_{-1}(F).\]
Since \(u\) admits a left inverse \(u'\), we necessarily have
\[y=u'(y',x)=g_*(y').\]
We must therefore show that \(y\) satisfies the relations (3.7.1).
To show that \(x=-i_*(y)\), we start from the relation \(f^*(x)=-j_*(y')\) and apply \(f_*\) to it. We thus obtain:
\[f_*(f^*(x))=-f_*(j_*(y'))=-i_*(g_*(y'))=-i_*(y)\]
by definition of \(y\). Now by proposition 3.6, \(f_*(f^*(x))=x\); whence the desired relation.
To complete the proof, we must show that
\[y'=g^*(g_*(y')).\lambda_{-1}(F).\]
Set \(z=y'-g^*(g_*(y')).\lambda_{-1}(F)\). We obtain:
\[g_*(z)=g_*(y')-g_*(y')g_*(\lambda_{-1}(F))=0.\]

On the other hand, we have by hypothesis: \(j_*(y')=-f^*(x)\), whence by what precedes \(j_*(y')=f^*(i_*(y))\), and by proposition 3.4: \(j_*(y')=j_*\bigl(g^*(y)\lambda_{-1}(F)\bigr)\); consequently, we also have \(j_*(z)=0\). Now \(\operatorname{Ker}(j_*)\cap\operatorname{Ker}(g_*)=0\). Indeed, if \(j_*(z)=0\), \(j^*(j_*(z))=0\); now \(j^*(j_*(z))=z(1-L)\) (prop. 2.7), and for \(z(1-L)=0\) to hold, there must exist \(z'\in K'(Y)\) such that \(z=z'\lambda_{-1}(F)\) (VI, 5.10); applying \(g_*\), this implies \(z'=g_*(z)=0\), hence \(z=0\).

Now assume \(X\) noetherian and \(Y\) regular. Then \(Y'\) is also regular since it is a projective bundle over \(Y\), and for every point \(y'\) of \(Y'\), the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X',y'}\) of \(X'\) at \(y'\) is a regular ring, since \(\mathcal O_{Y',y'}\) is a quotient of \(\mathcal O_{X',y'}\) by a regular element (see EGA OIV 17.1.8).

We know that for every regular scheme having an ample sheaf, the Grothendieck groups formed with the locally free sheaves of finite type and with the coherent sheaves are identical. This is therefore in particular the case for \(Y'\).

Let \(\underline E\) be a coherent \(\mathcal O_{X'}\)-Module with support in \(Y'\); then \(\underline E\) is a perfect \(\mathcal O_{X'}\)-Module. Indeed, it is so at the points of \(Y'\), since \(X'\) is regular at these points, and at the points of \(X'-Y'\) since it vanishes there.

If \(E\) is the class of \(\underline E\) in \(K'(X')\), then \(E\) belongs to the image of \(j_*\). Indeed, we can filter \(\underline E\) by the \(\underline J'{}^n.\underline E\). This filtration is finite, for, \(X'\) being noetherian, there exists an integer \(n_0\) such that \(\underline J'{}^{n_0}.\underline E=0\); the quotients of this filtration are then \(\mathcal O_{Y'}\)-Modules, so their class in \(K'(X')\) belongs to the image of \(j_*\). Since \(E\) is the sum of the classes of these quotients, \(E\) belongs to it as well.
Now let \(x\in K'(X')\). We can find a perfect complex \(M^{\bullet}\) whose class is \(x\). Consider the distinguished triangle:
\[ \xymatrix{ &N^{\bullet}\ar[dl]\ar[dr]&\\ M^{\bullet}\ar[rr]&&Lf^*(Rf_*(M^{\bullet})), } \]

the horizontal arrow being the canonical homomorphism. This arrow is an isomorphism on the complement of \(Y'\), and consequently the cohomology sheaves of \(N^{\bullet}\) are coherent sheaves with support in \(Y'\). By what precedes, they are perfect \(\mathcal O_{X'}\)-Modules, and their class belongs to the image of \(j_*\). We thus have:
\[x-f^*(f_*(x))\in\operatorname{Im}(j_*)\ ;\]
consequently, the homomorphism \(v\) is surjective.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 3.8.

In the case where \(X\) is noetherian and \(Y\) regular, 3.7 entirely determines the abelian group \(K'(X')\) from the \(\lambda\)-rings \(K'(X)\), \(K'(Y)\), the maps \(i_*\), \(i^*\) between these rings, and the element \(N\) of \(K'(Y)\); indeed, \(K'(Y')\) can be computed in terms of \(K'(Y)\) and \(N\) by (VI 1.1). We will see in the next section that the multiplicative structure of \(K'(X')\) is determined by the same data, as is the \(\lambda\)-structure if one tensors with \(\mathbb Q\) (this restriction being probably unnecessary: see 4.5).

pages.title: 4. Regular immersions and filtration of the \(K'\)

blocks.html: 4. Regular immersions and filtration of the \(K'\)
In this section we keep the notations and hypotheses of 3.1.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.1.

Suppose there exists on \(Y\) a locally free sheaf of finite type \(\underline N'\) such that, if \(N,N'\) are the classes in \(K'(Y)\) of \(\underline N\), \(\underline N'\), we have \(N=N'+2\). Then the following congruence holds:

\[\lambda_{-1}(F)\equiv0\pmod{(1-L)}.\]
Let \(d\) be the rank of \(\underline N\) (which we may assume constant); that of \(\underline F\) is then \(d-1\). By definition:
\[ \begin{aligned} \lambda_{-1}(F)&=\sum_{i\geq0}(-1)^i\lambda^i(F)\\ &=(-1)^{d-1}\lambda^{d-1}(F-1)\\ &=(-1)^{d-1}\lambda^{d-1}(F+L-1-L)\\ &=(-1)^{d-1}\lambda^{d-1}(N-2+1-L). \end{aligned} \]
Now \(\lambda_t(1-L)=\lambda_t(1)/\lambda_t(L)=(1+t)/(1+Lt)\). Hence for every \(i\geq1\), \(\lambda^i(1-L)\) is divisible by \(1-L\). Consequently:
\[ \begin{aligned} \lambda_{-1}(F)&\equiv(-1)^{d-1}\lambda^{d-1}(N-2)&&\pmod{(1-L)}\\ &\equiv(-1)^{d-1}\lambda^{d-1}(N')&&\pmod{(1-L)}. \end{aligned} \]
Now \(\underline N'\) is of rank \(d-2\), hence \(\lambda^{d-1}(N')=0\), whence the result.

4.2. Let \(i:Y\longrightarrow X\) be a regular immersion. Let \(r\) be an integer \(\geq1\), and \(s:X\longrightarrow\mathbb P_X^r\) the section defined by the homomorphism: \((\mathcal O_X)^{r+1}\longrightarrow\mathcal O_X\), passage to the quotient by the subsheaf generated by the last \(r\) coordinates. If \(\mathbb P_X^r\) is defined by the graded \(\mathcal O_X\)-Algebra \(\mathcal O_X[T_0,\ldots,T_r]\), the ideal \(I\) defining the section \(s\) is the ideal generated by the sections \(T_1,\ldots,T_r\), so \(I/I^2\) is isomorphic to \((\mathcal O_X[T_0])^r\), and hence the corresponding ideal of \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P}\) to \((\mathcal O_X)^r\).

Since the section \(s\) is obviously a regular immersion, the same holds for \(s\circ i\), and we can write the corresponding exact sequence of conormal sheaves (1.7). We thus obtain the following equality in \(K'(Y)\), denoting by \(\underline N\) the conormal sheaf of \(i\), and by \(\underline N_1\) the conormal sheaf of \(s\circ i\):
\[N_1=N+r.\]

We thus see that for every regular immersion \(i\), we can, by composing it with a section: \(X\longrightarrow\mathbb P_X^2\), or else: \(X\longrightarrow\mathbb P_{\mathbb P_X^1}^1\), obtain a regular immersion satisfying the property of lemma 4.1.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.3.

Let \(i:Y\longrightarrow X\) be a regular closed immersion, \(N\) the class in \(K'(Y)\) of the conormal sheaf of \(Y\) in \(X\). We assume that \(X\) is quasi-compact and has an ample sheaf. Then, with the notations of 3.3, we have for every \(y\in K'(Y)\), and every \(p\geq1\):

\[(4.3.1)\qquad i_*\bigl(\lambda^p(N,y)\bigr)\equiv\lambda^p(i_*(y))\pmod{\text{torsion}},\]

\(\lambda^p(N,y)\) being the element defined in (V 5.3).
i) We first assume that the hypothesis of 4.1 is satisfied; in this case we will show that \(i_*(\lambda^p(N,y))=\lambda^p(i_*(y))\). Since \(f_*\circ f^*=\operatorname{Id}\), this
relation is equivalent to the relation:
\[f^*\bigl(i_*(\lambda^p(N,y))\bigr)=f^*\bigl(\lambda^p(i_*(y))\bigr).\]
Now we have:
\[ \begin{aligned} f^*\bigl(\lambda^p(i_*(y))\bigr)&=\lambda^p\bigl(f^*(i_*(y))\bigr)\\ &=\lambda^p\bigl(j_*(g^*(y)\lambda_{-1}(F))\bigr)\qquad(3.4). \end{aligned} \]
On the other hand:
\[f^*\bigl(i_*(\lambda^p(N,y))\bigr)=j_*\bigl(g^*(\lambda^p(N,y))\lambda_{-1}(F)\bigr)\qquad(3.4).\]
Since \(g^*(N)=F+L\), we can apply the formula \(V (5.4.2)\):
\[g^*(\lambda^p(N,y))\lambda_{-1}(F)=\lambda^p(g^*(N),g^*(y))\lambda_{-1}(F)=\lambda^p(L,g^*(y)\lambda_{-1}(F)).\]
Setting: \(z=g^*(y)\lambda_{-1}(F)\), we are thus reduced to showing the relation:
\[j_*\bigl(\lambda^p(L,z)\bigr)=\lambda^p(j_*(z)),\]

that is to say, the analogous relation for the immersion \(j\), and the element \(z\). Now by hypothesis \(\lambda_{-1}(F)\) is a multiple of \((1-L)\); we can find \(z'\) such that \(z=z'(1-L)\). Then \(z=j^*(u')\) with \(u'=j_*(z')\) (2.7); since \(L=j^*(J')\), we finally have to verify:
\[j_*\bigl(j^*(\lambda^p(J',u'))\bigr)=\lambda^p\bigl(j_*(j^*(u'))\bigr).\]
The exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow\underline J'\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{X'}\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{Y'}\longrightarrow0\]
gives the relation:
\[(4.3.2)\qquad j_*(1)=1-J'.\]
The relation to be verified can therefore finally be written, by applying the projection formula:
\[\lambda^p(J',u')(1-J')=\lambda^p(u'(1-J')).\]
Since \(1-J'=\lambda_{-1}(J')\), this is the formula which serves to define \(\lambda^p(J',u')\).

ii) We now place ourselves in the general case. We then compose \(i\) with a section: \(X\longrightarrow\mathbb P_X^1\), then with a section: \(\mathbb P_X^1\longrightarrow\mathbb P_{\mathbb P_X^1}^1\), so that the composed immersion satisfies the condition of 4.1 (see 4.2). Proposition 4.3 is then true for the composed immersion by what precedes; it therefore remains to "descend back" to \(X\). We first descend back to \(\mathbb P_X^1\), then to \(X\), so that the proof is carried out by applying twice the following lemma (due to J.P. Jouanolou):

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma

Under the conditions of 4.3, let \(s:X\longrightarrow\mathbb P_X^1\) be the section defined in 4.2. If proposition 4.3 is true for \(s\circ i\), it is true for \(i\).
If we assume 4.3 true for \(s\circ i\), we have for every \(p\geq1\), and every \(y\in K'(Y)\):
\[(4.3.3)\qquad(s\circ i)_*\bigl(\lambda^p(N+1,y)\bigr)\equiv\lambda^p((s\circ i)_*(y))\pmod{\text{torsion}}.\]

The section \(s\) is defined by an ideal isomorphic to \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P}(-1)\) (4.2); we denote by \(\xi\) its class in \(K'(\mathbb P_X^1)\). We then have the following relation for every \(x\in K'(X)\):
\[s_*(x)=p^*(x)(1-\xi),\]

\(p\) being the structural morphism \(\mathbb P_X^1\longrightarrow X\). Indeed, since \(p\circ s=\operatorname{Id}_X\), we have \(x=s^*(p^*(x))\), whence \(s_*(x)=s_*(s^*(p^*(x)))=p^*(x)s_*(1)\); since \(s_*(1)=1-\xi\), we have the desired relation:
The relation (4.3.3) can then be written:

\[ \begin{aligned} (s\circ i)_*\bigl(\lambda^p(N+1,y)\bigr)&\equiv\lambda^p\bigl(p^*(i_*(y))(1-\xi)\bigr)&&\pmod{\text{torsion}}\\ &\equiv\lambda^p\bigl(\xi,p^*(i_*(y))\bigr)(1-\xi)&&\pmod{\text{torsion}}. \end{aligned} \]

Now \(\lambda^p(\xi,p^*(i_*(y)))-\lambda^p(1,p^*(i_*(y)))\) is divisible by \(1-\xi\); to see this, we can place ourselves in the universal \(\lambda\)-ring which serves to define the \(\lambda^p(\xi,x)\) (V 5); then \(\lambda^p(\xi,x)-\lambda^p(1,x)\) is a polynomial in \(\xi\), zero for \(\xi=1\), hence divisible by \(\xi-1\). Moreover, the equation of dependence of \(\xi\) over \(K'(X)\) is \((1-\xi)^2=0\) \(VI (1.13.1)\). We thus obtain:

\[\begin{aligned}(s\circ i)_*\bigl(\lambda^p(N+1,y)\bigr)&\equiv\lambda^p\bigl(1,p^*(i_*(y))\bigr)(1-\xi)&&\pmod{\text{torsion}}\\&\equiv p^*\bigl(\lambda^p(1,i_*(y))\bigr)(1-\xi)&&\pmod{\text{torsion}}.\end{aligned}\]
Applying \(p_*\), we obtain:
\[(4.3.4)\qquad i_*\bigl(\lambda^p(N+1,y)\bigr)\equiv\lambda^p(1,i_*(y))\pmod{\text{torsion}},\]
By the formula of (V 5.6), applied to \(L=1\), \(\lambda^p(N+1,y)\) is the coefficient of \(t^p\) in the series:
\[\frac{\sum_{k\geq1}\lambda^k(N,y)kt^k}{1+\sum_{k\geq1}\lambda^k(N,y)\lambda_{-1}(N)t^k}.\]
Likewise, the same formula applied to \(N=0\) and \(L=1\) shows that \(\lambda^p(1,y)\) is the coefficient of \(t^p\) in the series:
\[\frac{\sum_{k\geq1}\lambda^k(y)kt^k}{\sum_{k\geq0}\lambda^k(y)t^k}.\]
The congruences (4.3.4) can therefore be written:

\[i_*\left(\frac{\sum_{k\geq1}k\lambda^k(N,y)t^k}{1+\sum_{k\geq1}\lambda^k(N,y)\lambda_{-1}(N)t^k}\right)\equiv\frac{\sum_{k\geq1}k\lambda^k(i_*(y))t^k}{\sum_{k\geq0}\lambda^k(i_*(y))t^k}\pmod{\text{torsion}}.\]
Applying formula 2.8, we obtain:

\[\frac{\sum_{k\geq0}ki_*\bigl(\lambda^k(N,y)\bigr)t^k}{1+\sum_{k\geq1}i_*\bigl(\lambda^k(N,y)\bigr)t^k}\equiv\frac{\sum_{k\geq0}k\lambda^k(i_*(y))t^k}{\sum_{k\geq0}\lambda^k(i_*(y))t^k}\pmod{\text{torsion}}.\]
If we set:
\[u(t)=1+\sum_{k\geq1}i_*\bigl(\lambda^k(N,y)\bigr)t^k,\qquad v(t)=\sum_{k\geq0}\lambda^k(i_*(y))t^k,\]
this last relation can also be written:
\[\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{u(t)}{v(t)}\right)\equiv0\pmod{\text{torsion}}.\]
Since the constant term of \(u(t)/v(t)\) is 1, and we are writing congruences modulo torsion, we can integrate and write the congruence \(u(t)\equiv v(t)\). This gives the announced congruences.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.4.

With the notations and hypotheses of 4.3, we have for every \(p\geq1\), and every \(y\in K'(Y)\):

\[i_*\bigl(\gamma^p(N,y)\bigr)\equiv\gamma^p(i_*(y))\pmod{\text{torsion}}.\]
The corollary follows immediately from 4.3, using the expression of the \(\gamma^p\) as linear combinations with integer coefficients of the \(\lambda^p\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.5.

The very proof of 4.3 suggests that (4.3.1) must be true not only modulo torsion, but also as an equality. In [RRR], the equality was shown in characteristic 0 thanks to a geometric characterization of the \(\lambda^p(N,x)\). An extension of this method to all characteristics would probably be possible if one knew how to define the exterior power operations on perfect complexes (cf. XIV).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.6.

Let \(X\) be a quasi-compact scheme having an ample sheaf, \(d\) an integer \(>0\) and \(i:Y\longrightarrow X\) a regular immersion of codimension \(d\). Then, for every \(k\), we have:

\[i_*\bigl((\operatorname{Fil}^kK'(Y))\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Q\bigr)\subset(\operatorname{Fil}^{k+d}K'(X))\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Q.\]

Let us first remark that \(i_*(K'(Y))\subset\operatorname{Fil}^1K'(X)\). Indeed, \(X-Y\) is dense in \(X\): if this were not so, \(Y\) would contain an affine open set, and on this open set, the ideal \(\underline J\) defining \(Y\) would be a nil ideal, hence a locally nilpotent ideal since it is of finite type. Now this is impossible, since the \(\underline J^n/\underline J^{n+1}\) are locally free \(\mathcal O_Y\)-Modules of rank \(\ne0\). The augmentation in \(K'(X)\) of an element of \(i_*(K'(Y))\) is a locally constant function on \(X\); being zero on \(X-Y\), it is zero on all of \(X\).
For every abelian group \(A\), we will set \(A_{\mathbb Q}=A\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Q\). Let \(x\in\operatorname{Fil}^kK'(Y)_{\mathbb Q}\); we can write \(x\) in the form:
\[x=\sum a\gamma^{i_1}(x_1)\cdots\gamma^{i_n}(x_n),\]

with \(\varepsilon(x_i)=0\) for every \(i\), \(a\in\mathbb Q\), and \(i_1+\cdots+i_n\geq k\). The \(x_i\) can be expressed as combinations with integer coefficients of the classes \(E_\alpha\) of a finite number of locally free \(\mathcal O_Y\)-Modules of finite type \(\underline E_\alpha\). Let \(R\) be the sub-\(\mathbb Q\)-\(\lambda\)-algebra of \(K'(Y)_{\mathbb Q}\) generated by the \(E_\alpha\) and by \(N\). We then have:
\[x\in\operatorname{Fil}^kR.\]

Moreover, the \(\lambda\)-ring filtration of \(R\) is discrete (VI 6.2); let \(m\) be such that \(\operatorname{Fil}^mR=0\). Finally, we may, by decomposing \(X\) into a finite sum of disjoint open sets and carrying out the proof in each open set if necessary (the \(K'\) commuting with finite direct sums), assume the \(\underline E_\alpha\) of constant rank \(r_\alpha\). The augmentation ring of \(R\) is then \(\mathbb Q\).
We will show that for every \(i\), we have:
\[i_*(\operatorname{Fil}^iR)\subset\operatorname{Fil}^{i+d}(K'(X)_{\mathbb Q}).\]
This will imply in particular:
\[i_*(x)\in\operatorname{Fil}^{k+d}K'(X),\]
and will therefore prove the theorem.
The proof is by descending induction on \(i\), the case \(i=m\) being trivial. Let \(y\in\operatorname{Fil}^iR\), and set for every \(j\):
\[a_j=(-1)^{j-1}(j-1)!\ .\]
Then by V 6.10,
\[\gamma^{d+i}(N,y)-a_{d+i}y\in\operatorname{Fil}^{i+1}R.\]
We can therefore apply the induction hypothesis, assuming that:
\[i_*(\operatorname{Fil}^{i+1}R)\subset\operatorname{Fil}^{i+d+1}K'(X)_{\mathbb Q}.\]
We consequently have:
\[i_*\bigl(\gamma^{d+i}(N,y)-a_{d+i}y\bigr)\in\operatorname{Fil}^{i+d+1}K'(X)_{\mathbb Q}.\]
By 4.4, this implies:
\[\gamma^{d+i}(i_*(y))-a_{d+i}i_*(y)\in\operatorname{Fil}^{i+d+1}K'(X)_{\mathbb Q}.\]
Since \(i_*(y)\) has zero augmentation, \(\gamma^{d+i}(i_*(y))\in\operatorname{Fil}^{d+i}K'(X)\). Since we are computing modulo torsion, we thus find:
\[i_*(y)\in\operatorname{Fil}^{i+d}K'(X)_{\mathbb Q}.\]

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.6.1.

We see that, even if 4.3 were true without tensoring with \(\mathbb Q\), the proof above only works modulo torsion. For a counterexample, see XIV 4.6.
4.7. We return to the situation of 3.7, where we had the commutative diagram:
\[\begin{array}{ccc}Y'&\xrightarrow{j}&X'\\\downarrow g&&\downarrow f\\Y&\xrightarrow{i}&X,\end{array}\]

where \(i\) is a regular immersion, \(X'\) the \(X\)-scheme obtained by blowing up \(Y\), and \(Y'=X'\times_XY\). We assume \(X\) noetherian with an ample sheaf, and \(Y\) regular. We saw in 3.7 that we then have the exact sequence:
\[0\longrightarrow K'(Y)\xrightarrow{u}K'(Y')\times K'(X)\xrightarrow{v}K'(X')\longrightarrow0.\]

We are going to show that this exact sequence makes it possible to describe the whole situation formed by the \(K'\) of \(X\), \(Y\), \(X'\), \(Y'\) from the data of the \(\lambda\)-rings \(K'(X)\), \(K'(Y)\), the maps \(i^*\) and \(i_*\), and the element \(N\) of \(K'(Y)\) (at least up to torsion, until further notice).

Let then \(K\), \(K'\) be two \(\lambda\)-rings, \(i_*:K\longrightarrow K'\) an additive homomorphism, \(i^*:K'\longrightarrow K\) a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism, \(d\) an integer, and \(N\) an element of \(K\) such that \(\lambda^i(N)=0\) for \(i>d\) and such that \(\lambda^d(N)\) is invertible. We further assume the following properties to be true:
i) \(i_*\) is \(K'\)-linear (\(\text{« formule de projection »}\)) ;
ii) \(i^*\circ i_*\) is the homomorphism \(\text{« multiplication par }\lambda_{-1}(N)\text{ »}\) ;
iii) for every \(n\geq1\), and every \(y\in K\), \(i_*\bigl(\lambda^n(N,y)\bigr)=\lambda^n(i_*(y))\) \(relation (4.3.1)\).

We first use VI.1.1 to construct a \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-algebra \(K_1\) which plays the role of \(K'(Y')\). Let \(L\) be an indeterminate, and \(K[L]\) the \(K\)-\(\lambda\)-algebra generated by the generator \(L\) subject to the relations \(\lambda^i(L)=0\) for \(i\geq2\) (V 4.9). Let:
\[P(L)=\lambda^d(N-L)=\sum_{i=0}^d(-1)^i\lambda^{d-i}(N)L^i.\]

The ideal generated by \(P\) is a \(\lambda\)-ideal. To see this, we first remark that the relations \(\lambda^i(N)=0\) for \(i>d\) imply that the \(\lambda^j(N-L)\) for \(j\geq d\) are multiples of \(\lambda^d(N-L)\). If we place ourselves in \(1+K/(P)[[t]]^+\), the image of \(\lambda_t(N-L)\) is a polynomial of degree \(<d\), and a universal argument then shows that its \(\lambda^i\) for \(i\geq d\) are equal to 1 (cf. V 4.8). Since in \(1+K[[t]]^+\) we have \(\lambda_t(\lambda^d(N-L))=\lambda^d(\lambda_t(N-L))\), we deduce that the \(\lambda^i(\lambda^d(N-L))\) for \(i\geq1\) are multiples of \(P\). By the relations V 2.4.2, this implies that the ideal generated by \(P\) is a \(\lambda\)-ideal.

We then set \(K_1=K/(P)\). The natural homomorphism \(K\longrightarrow K_1\) will be denoted by \(g^*\); it is a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism. On the other hand, \(K_1\) is a free \(K\)-module with basis the elements \(1,L,\ldots,L^{d-1}\). It follows from the relation \(\lambda^d(N)=\lambda^{d-1}(N-L).L\) and from the invertibility hypothesis made on \(\lambda^d(N)\) that \(L\) is invertible.
