Consequently \(K_1\) has as basis over \(K\) the elements \(1,L^{-1},\ldots,L^{-d+1}\); we then define a map \(g_*:K_1\longrightarrow K\) by associating to every element \(y'=y_0+y_1L^{-1}+\cdots+y_{d-1}L^{-d+1}\) of \(K_1\) the element \(y_0\) of \(K\). It is clear that the map thus defined is an additive homomorphism, \(K\)-linear, and such that \(g_*g^*=\operatorname{Id}\).
We next equip \(K_1\times K'\) with a \(\lambda\)-ring structure, by setting:
\[(4.7.1)\qquad(y'_1,x_1)*(y'_2,x_2)=\bigl(y'_1y'_2(1-L)+x_1y'_2+x_2y'_1,\;x_1x_2\bigr)\ ;\]
\[(4.7.2)\qquad\lambda_*^n(y',x)=\left(\sum_{p=1}^n\lambda^{n-p}(y')\lambda^p(L,y'),\;\lambda^n(x)\right)\ .\]
In these formulas, \(K_1\) is regarded as a \(K'\)-algebra via the homomorphism \(g^*\circ i^*\).
We then set, for \(y_1,y_2\in K\) and \(n\geq1\):
\[(4.7.3)\qquad y_1*y_2=-y_1y_2\lambda_{-1}(N)\ ;\qquad(4.7.4)\qquad\lambda_*^n(y_1)=-\lambda^n(N,-y_1)\ ;\]
and we define a homomorphism \(u:K\longrightarrow K_1\times K'\) by setting:
\[u(y)=\bigl(g^*(y).\lambda_{-1}(F),-i_*(y)\bigr)\ ,\]
with \(F=N-L\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.7.5.

The operations defined by (4.7.1) and (4.7.2) equip \(K_1\times K'\) with a \(\lambda\)-ring structure, and \(u\) is an injective homomorphism which identifies \(K\), equipped with the operations defined by (4.7.3) and (4.7.4), with a \(\lambda\)-ideal of \(K_1\times K'\).

It is immediate to verify that \(K_1\times K'\) is a \(\lambda\)-ring; one may refer for example to V 5.5, where an entirely analogous construction is carried out. The proof of the second part of the proposition is likewise done without difficulty by using the preceding relations, the relation \(i_*(y_1y_2\lambda_{-1}(N))=i_*(y_1).i_*(y_2)\) which follows immediately from the hypotheses, the relation \(g_*(\lambda_{-1}(F))=1\) (see VI 5.9) and the relations of V 5.
We then set \(K'_1=(K_1\times K')/K\) and denote by \(v\) the canonical homomorphism \(K_1\times K'\longrightarrow K'_1\). We regard \(K'_1\) as a \(\lambda\)-ring by means of the quotient structure.
We next define additive homomorphisms:
\[j_*:K_1\longrightarrow K'_1\ ;\quad j^*:K'_1\longrightarrow K_1\ ;\qquad f_*:K'_1\longrightarrow K'\ ;\quad f^*:K'\longrightarrow K'_1\ .\]

The homomorphism \(j_*\) is the composite \(K_1\longrightarrow K_1\times K'\longrightarrow K'_1\). Likewise, the homomorphism \(f^*\) is the composite \(K'\longrightarrow K_1\times K'\longrightarrow K'_1\). We then have: \(v=j_*+f^*\).

We next define a homomorphism \(K_1\times K'\longrightarrow K_1\) by associating to \((y',x)\) the element \(y'(1-L)+g^*\circ i^*(x)\); this homomorphism vanishes on \(K\), and consequently defines a homomorphism \(j^*:K'_1\longrightarrow K_1\). Finally, we define a homomorphism \(K_1\times K'\longrightarrow K'\) by associating to \((y',x)\) the element \(i_*\circ g_*(y')+x\); this homomorphism vanishes on \(K\) and therefore defines a homomorphism \(f_*:K'_1\longrightarrow K'\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.7.6.

i) \(j^*\) and \(f^*\) are \(\lambda\)-homomorphisms ;

ii) \(j_*\) is \(K'_1\)-linear and \(f_*\) is \(K'\)-linear ;

iii) \(j^*\circ j_*\) is the homomorphism "multiplication by \(1-L\)" ;

iv) for every \(y'\in K_1\), and every \(p\geq1\), \(j_*(\lambda^p(L,y'))=\lambda^p(j_*(y'))\) ;

v) \(f_*\circ f^*=\operatorname{Id}\).
It suffices to turn the crank.

If we return to the geometric situation recalled above, we see that \(K'(X')\) is constructed from \(K'(Y)\), \(K'(X)\), the maps \(i^*\) and \(i_*\), and the element \(N\) of \(K'(Y)\) by the preceding method, the operations \(\lambda\) on \(K'(X')\) however coinciding with the operations \(\lambda\) defined above on \(K'_1\) only up to torsion, as long as the relation (4.3.1) is proved only modulo torsion.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.8.

Let \(i:Y\longrightarrow X\) be a regular closed immersion of constant codimension \(d\), \(X'\) the scheme obtained by blowing up \(Y\), and \(Y'=X'\times_XY\), so that we have the commutative diagram:

\[\begin{array}{ccc}Y'&\xrightarrow{j}&X'\\\downarrow g&&\downarrow f\\Y&\xrightarrow{i}&X.\end{array}\]

We assume that \(X\) is quasi-compact and has an ample sheaf. Then we have for every \(k\) the exact sequence:

\[0\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}^{k-d}K'(Y)_{\mathbb Q}\xrightarrow{u^{\mathrm{gr}}}\operatorname{Gr}^{k-1}K'(Y')_{\mathbb Q}\times\operatorname{Gr}^kK'(X)_{\mathbb Q}\xrightarrow{v^{\mathrm{gr}}}\operatorname{Gr}^kK'(X')_{\mathbb Q}\]

where we set, for \(y\in\operatorname{Gr}^{k-d}K'(Y)_{\mathbb Q}\):

\[u^{\mathrm{gr}}(y)=\bigl(g^{\mathrm{gr}}(y).(-1)^{d-1}c^{d-1}(F),-i_{\mathrm{gr}}(y)\bigr)\ ,\]

and for \(y'\in\operatorname{Gr}^{k-1}K'(Y')_{\mathbb Q}\) and \(x\in\operatorname{Gr}^kK'(X)_{\mathbb Q}\):

\[v^{\mathrm{gr}}(y',x)=j_{\mathrm{gr}}(y')+f^{\mathrm{gr}}(x)\ ,\]

the notations being those of 3.1 and 3.3, and \(c^{d-1}(F)\) denoting the \((d-1)\)-th Chern class of \(F\) (V 6.7). Moreover, \(u^{\mathrm{gr}}\) admits a left inverse \(u'^{\mathrm{gr}}\), defined by \(u'^{\mathrm{gr}}(y',x)=g_{\mathrm{gr}}(y')\).

If in addition \(X\) is noetherian and \(Y\) regular, we have the exact sequence:

\[0\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}^{k-d}K'(Y)_{\mathbb Q}\xrightarrow{u^{\mathrm{gr}}}\operatorname{Gr}^{k-1}K'(Y')_{\mathbb Q}\times\operatorname{Gr}^kK'(X)_{\mathbb Q}\xrightarrow{v^{\mathrm{gr}}}\operatorname{Gr}^kK'(X')_{\mathbb Q}\longrightarrow0.\]

Proof. Note that in the case where \(Y\) is regular one could give a purely algebraic proof, in terms of the theory of \(\lambda\)-rings, using the description 4.7 of the situation. Instead, we are going to model the proof on that of 3.7.

We know that the "inverse image" homomorphisms \(f^*\), \(g^*\), being \(\lambda\)-homomorphisms, are compatible with the \(\lambda\)-ring filtrations, and consequently they define homomorphisms on the associated graded objects, whence the homomorphisms:
\[f^{\mathrm{gr}}:\operatorname{Gr}^kK'(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}^kK'(X')\ ;\quad g^{\mathrm{gr}}:\operatorname{Gr}^{k-d}K'(Y)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}^{k-d}K'(Y')\ ,\]

and the analogous homomorphisms obtained after tensoring with \(\mathbb Q\). Moreover, 4.6 shows that after tensoring with \(\mathbb Q\), the homomorphisms \(i_*\), \(j_*\) pass to the associated graded objects, and therefore define:

\[i_{\mathrm{gr}}:\operatorname{Gr}^{k-d}K'(Y)_{\mathbb Q}\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}^kK'(X)_{\mathbb Q}\ ;\quad j_{\mathrm{gr}}:\operatorname{Gr}^{k-1}K'(Y')_{\mathbb Q}\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}^kK'(X')_{\mathbb Q}\ .\]

Finally, we remark that \(\lambda_{-1}(F)=(-1)^{d-1}\gamma^{d-1}(F)\), hence that the morphisms \(u^{\mathrm{gr}}\) and \(v^{\mathrm{gr}}\) are obtained by passage to the quotient from the morphisms \(u\) and \(v\) of 3.7. We thus have \(v^{\mathrm{gr}}\circ u^{\mathrm{gr}}=0\).
By VI 5.8, \(g_*(\operatorname{Fil}^{k-1}K'(Y'))\subset\operatorname{Fil}^{k-d}K'(Y)\) for every \(k\), since \(Y'=\mathbb P(N)\), \(N\) being of rank \(d\). We thus deduce a homomorphism:
\[g_{\mathrm{gr}}:\operatorname{Gr}^{k-1}K'(Y')\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}^{k-d}K'(Y)\ ,\]

and an analogous homomorphism after tensoring with \(\mathbb Q\). The homomorphism \(u'^{\mathrm{gr}}\) is thus obtained from \(u'\) (3.7) by passage to the associated graded object. Since \(u'\) is a left inverse of \(u\), \(u'^{\mathrm{gr}}\) is a left inverse of \(u^{\mathrm{gr}}\), which at the same time proves the injectivity of \(u^{\mathrm{gr}}\).

To show that \(\operatorname{Ker}(v^{\mathrm{gr}})=\operatorname{Im}(u^{\mathrm{gr}})\), we must show that if we have \(y'\in\operatorname{Fil}^{k-1}K'(Y')_{\mathbb Q}\) and \(x\in\operatorname{Fil}^kK'(X)_{\mathbb Q}\), such that:
\[(4.6.1)\qquad j_*(y')+f^*(x)\in\operatorname{Fil}^{k+1}K'(X')_{\mathbb Q}\ ,\]
then there exists \(y\in\operatorname{Fil}^{k-d}K'(Y)_{\mathbb Q}\) such that:
i) \(x+i_*(y)\in\operatorname{Fil}^{k+1}K'(X)_{\mathbb Q}\),
ii) \(y'-g^*(y)\lambda_{-1}(F)\in\operatorname{Fil}^kK'(Y')_{\mathbb Q}\).

Set: \(y=g_*(y')\). Then by VI 5.8, \(y\in\operatorname{Fil}^{k-d}K'(Y)\). We next remark that \(f_*\) preserves the filtration (using results from Exposé VIII, which causes no problem, since 4.8 is not used to prove that fact). Indeed, it follows from 1.8 ii) that \(f\) is a complete intersection morphism of virtual relative dimension zero (VIII 4.2). Since \(f\) is projective, the result is a consequence of VIII 3.2. Applying \(f_*\) to the relation
(4.8.1), we obtain:
\[f_*(j_*(y'))+f_*(f^*(x))\in\operatorname{Fil}^{k+1}K'(X)_{\mathbb Q},\]
that is, by 3.6:
\[i_*(y)+x\in\operatorname{Fil}^{k+1}K'(X)_{\mathbb Q}.\]
To show the relation ii), we must show that:
\[y'-g^*(g_*(y'))\lambda_{-1}(F)\in\operatorname{Fil}^kK'(Y')_{\mathbb Q}.\]
Set \(z=y'-g^*(g_*(y'))\lambda_{-1}(F)\). We have:
\[\begin{aligned}j_*(z)&=j_*(y')-j_*(g^*(g_*(y'))\lambda_{-1}(F))\\&=j_*(y')-f^*(i_*(y))\quad\text{d'après 3.4.}\end{aligned}\]
Now the relation i) gives:
\[f^*(i_*(y))+f^*(x)\in\operatorname{Fil}^{k+1}K'(X')_{\mathbb Q}.\]
Taking (4.6.1) into account, we thus obtain:
\[j_*(z)\in\operatorname{Fil}^{k+1}K'(X')_{\mathbb Q},\]
whence:
\[j^*(j_*(z))=z(1-L)\in\operatorname{Fil}^{k+1}K'(Y')_{\mathbb Q}.\]

Let \(\bar z\) be the image of \(z\) in \(\operatorname{Gr}^{k-1}K'(Y')_{\mathbb Q}\), and \(\overline{(1-L)}\) that of \((1-L)\) in \(\operatorname{Gr}^1K'(Y')_{\mathbb Q}\). We thus have: \(\bar z\,\overline{(1-L)}=0\). We can then apply (VI 5.11) (which remains valid after tensoring with \(\mathbb Q\), since its proof relies only on the structure of \(K'(Y')\) as a free \(K'(Y)\)-algebra), and we can therefore find \(z'\in K'(Y)_{\mathbb Q}\) such that:
\[z-g^*(z')\lambda_{-1}(F)\in\operatorname{Fil}^kK'(Y')_{\mathbb Q}.\]
Applying \(g_*\), we find, taking into account that \(g_*(z)=0\):
\[z'\in\operatorname{Fil}^{k+1-d}K'(Y)_{\mathbb Q},\]
whence, since \(\lambda_{-1}(F)\in\operatorname{Fil}^{d-1}K'(Y')_{\mathbb Q}\):
\[z\in\operatorname{Fil}^kK'(Y')_{\mathbb Q},\]
which completes the proof.
Now assume \(X\) noetherian and \(Y\) regular, which implies that \(v\) is surjective (3.7). We saw in the proof of 3.7 that for every \(x'\in K'(X')\) there exists \(y'\in K'(Y')\) such that:
\[x'=f^*(f_*(x'))+j_*(y').\]

The same property obviously remains true after tensoring with \(\mathbb Q\). Consequently, if \(x'\in\operatorname{Fil}^kK'(X')_{\mathbb Q}\), there exist \((y',x)\in K'(Y')_{\mathbb Q}\times K'(X)_{\mathbb Q}\) such that:
\[x'=v(y',x),\qquad x\in\operatorname{Fil}^kK'(X)_{\mathbb Q}.\]
Indeed, it suffices to take \(x=f_*(x')\).
We filter the ring \(K'(Y')_{\mathbb Q}\times K'(X)_{\mathbb Q}\) defined in 4.7 by setting:
\[\operatorname{Fil}^k(K'(Y')_{\mathbb Q}\times K'(X)_{\mathbb Q})=\operatorname{Fil}^{k-1}K'(Y')_{\mathbb Q}\times\operatorname{Fil}^kK'(X)_{\mathbb Q}.\]
It follows immediately from (4.7.1) that this is a ring filtration.
To show that for every \(k\) the homomorphism:
\[\operatorname{Fil}^k(K'(Y')_{\mathbb Q}\times K'(X)_{\mathbb Q})\xrightarrow{v}\operatorname{Fil}^kK'(X')_{\mathbb Q}\]

is surjective, it therefore suffices to show that if \(x'=\gamma^k(x'_1)\), with \(\varepsilon(x'_1)=0\), there exists \((y',x)\in\operatorname{Fil}^k(K'(Y')_{\mathbb Q}\times K'(X)_{\mathbb Q})\) such that \(x'=v(y',x)\).

Now by what precedes, there exists \((y'_1,x_1)\in K'(Y')_{\mathbb Q}\times K'(X)_{\mathbb Q}\) such that \(x'_1=v(y'_1,x_1)\) and \(x_1\in\operatorname{Fil}^1K'(X)_{\mathbb Q}\). By 4.7, we then have:
\[x'=v(\gamma_*^k(y'_1,x_1)).\]
Now:
\[\gamma_*^k(y'_1,x_1)=\left(\sum_{p=1}^k\gamma^{k-p}(x_1)\gamma^p(L,y'_1),\ \gamma^k(x_1)\right)\]
(an immediate consequence of (4.7.2)). Since \(x_1\) has zero augmentation, \(\gamma^k(x_1)\in\operatorname{Fil}^kK'(X)_{\mathbb Q}\), and taking V 5.7 into account,
\[\sum_{p=1}^k\gamma^{k-p}(x_1)\gamma^p(L,y'_1)\in\operatorname{Fil}^{k-1}K'(Y')_{\mathbb Q},\]
whence the result.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.9.

In the case where \(X\) is noetherian and \(Y\) regular, one can, by proceeding as in 4.7, describe the graded ring structure of \(\operatorname{Gr}^*K'(X')_{\mathbb Q}\) in terms of the graded rings \(\operatorname{Gr}^*K'(X)_{\mathbb Q}\), \(\operatorname{Gr}^*K'(Y)_{\mathbb Q}\), the additive homomorphisms \(i^{\mathrm{gr}}\) and \(i_{\mathrm{gr}}\) deduced from \(i^*\) and \(i_*\), and the element \(c^.(N)\) of \(\operatorname{Gr}^*K'(Y)\). Likewise, one can describe from the same data the homomorphisms \(f^{\mathrm{gr}}\), \(f_{\mathrm{gr}}\), \(j^{\mathrm{gr}}\), \(j_{\mathrm{gr}}\) defined from \(f^*\), \(f_*\), \(j^*\), \(j_*\) by passage to the associated graded objects.

4.10. Let \(X\) be a regular scheme having an ample sheaf. We know (IV ) that the Grothendieck groups formed with the locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-Modules of finite type, and with the coherent \(\mathcal O_X\)-Modules, are isomorphic. We use this fact to define on \(K'(X)\) a "topological" filtration, by taking for \(\operatorname{Fil}_{\mathrm{top}}^iK'(X)\) the subgroup generated by the classes of the coherent sheaves whose support is of codimension \(\geq i\); one easily shows that \(\operatorname{Fil}_{\mathrm{top}}^iK'(X)\) is generated by the classes of the sheaves \(\mathcal O_Z\), where \(Z\) runs over the set of integral closed subschemes of \(X\) of codimension \(\geq i\) (X ).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.11.

Let \(X\) be a regular scheme having an ample sheaf. Then for every \(k\geq0\):

\[\operatorname{Fil}_{\mathrm{top}}^kK'(X)_{\mathbb Q}\subset\operatorname{Fil}_{\lambda}^kK'(X)_{\mathbb Q}.\]

(N.B. The reverse inclusion, valid without tensoring with \(\mathbb Q\), will be proved in X ).
By 4.10, it suffices to show that if \(Z\) is an integral closed subscheme of \(X\), of codimension \(k\), then:
\[\operatorname{cl}(\mathcal O_Z)\in\operatorname{Fil}^kK'(X)_{\mathbb Q}.\]

For this, we proceed by noetherian induction on the integral closed subschemes of \(X\). Assume therefore the property proved for the integral closed subschemes of \(Z\), and let us show it for \(Z\). Let \(J\) be the Ideal of \(\mathcal O_X\)

defining \(Z\), and let \(z\) be the generic point of \(Z\). Since \(Z\) is integral, \(J_z\) is the maximal ideal of \(\mathcal O_{X,z}\), and, since \(X\) is regular, \(J_z\) can be generated by an \(\mathcal O_{X,z}\)-regular sequence. We can therefore find a sequence of sections of \(J\) in a neighborhood of \(z\), forming an \(\mathcal O_X\)-regular sequence. Consequently, there exists an open neighborhood \(U\) of \(z\), such that \(Z'=X-U\) is contained in \(Z\), and such that \(J|U\) is a regular ideal of \(\mathcal O_U\). The immersion \(Z\cap U\xrightarrow{i}U\) is then a regular immersion, whose codimension is \(\dim(\mathcal O_{X,z})=\operatorname{codim}(Z,X)\) (EGA IV 5.1.2). Moreover, we have the exact sequence (IV ):
\[K'(Z')\xrightarrow{j_*}K'(X)\xrightarrow{u^*}K'(U)\longrightarrow0,\]

where \(j\) is the immersion \(Z'\longrightarrow X\) and \(u\) the immersion \(U\longrightarrow X\); we obtain an analogous sequence after tensoring with \(\mathbb Q\). Now \(u^*(\mathcal O_Z)=i_*(\mathcal O_{Z\cap U})\), and by 4.6, the image \(u^*(1_Z)\) of the class of \(\mathcal O_Z\) under \(u^*\) is therefore in \(\operatorname{Fil}^kK'(U)_{\mathbb Q}\). Since \(u^*\) is a surjective \(\lambda\)-homomorphism, it induces a surjection
\[\operatorname{Fil}^kK'(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Fil}^kK'(U),\]
hence \(u^*(1_Z)=u^*(x)\) with \(x\in\operatorname{Fil}^kK'(X)\); there thus exists \(y\in j_*(K'(Z'))\) such that:
\[\operatorname{cl}(\mathcal O_Z)-y\in\operatorname{Fil}^kK'(X)_{\mathbb Q}.\]

Now \(Z'\subset Z\), and since \(z\notin Z'\), \(\operatorname{codim}(Z',X)>k\). Consequently, \(y\) can be expressed as a combination of classes of sheaves \(\mathcal O_{Z''_\alpha}\), where the \(Z''_\alpha\) are integral closed subschemes of \(Z\), distinct from \(Z\). By the induction hypothesis, \(y\in\operatorname{Fil}^{k+1}K'(X)_{\mathbb Q}\), which completes the proof.
BIBLIOGRAPHY
